Recent advancements in large vision-language models (VLMs) have primarily focused on English, with limited attention given to other languages. To address this gap, we introduce MEENA (also known as PersianMMMU), the first dataset designed to evaluate Persian VLMs across scientific, reasoning, and human-level understanding tasks. Our dataset comprises approximately 7,500 Persian and 3,000 English questions, covering a wide range of topics such as reasoning, mathematics, physics, diagrams, charts, and Persian art and literature. Key features of MEENA include: (1) diverse subject coverage spanning various educational levels, from primary to upper secondary school, (2) rich metadata, including difficulty levels and descriptive answers, (3) original Persian data that preserves cultural nuances, (4) a bilingual structure to assess cross-linguistic performance, and (5) a series of diverse experiments assessing various capabilities, including overall performance, the model’s ability to attend to images, and its tendency to generate hallucinations. We hope this benchmark contributes to enhancing VLM capabilities beyond English.

Tokenizer adaptation plays an important role in adapting pre-trained language models to new domains or languages. In this work, we address two complementary aspects of this process: vocabulary extension and pruning. The common approach to extension trains a new tokenizer on domain-specific text and appends the tokens that do not overlap with the existing vocabulary, which often results in many tokens that are unreachable or never used. We propose continued BPE training that extends a pre-trained tokenizer by continuing the BPE merge learning process on new data. Experiments across multiple languages and model families show that this approach improves tokenization efficiency and leads to better utilization of added vocabulary. We also introduce leaf-based vocabulary pruning, which removes redundant tokens while preserving model quality. Together, these methods provide practical tools for controlled vocabulary modification, which we release as an open-source toolkit.

Despite significant progress in image captioning, generating accurate and descriptive captions remains a long-standing challenge. In this study, we propose Attention-Guided Image Captioning (AGIC), which amplifies salient visual regions directly in the feature space to guide caption generation. Moreover, AGIC demonstrates strong performance across multiple evaluation metrics, offering a scalable and interpretable solution for image captioning.

**[Visual–Linguistic Abductive Reasoning with LLMs for Knowledge-based Visual Question Answering](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.343/)**

[Jieun Kim](https://aclanthology.org/people/jieun-kim/unverified/)
\|
[Yujin Jeong](https://aclanthology.org/people/yujin-jeong/unverified/)
\|
[Sung-Bae Cho](https://aclanthology.org/people/sung-bae-cho/)

Recent attempts to leverage large language models (LLMs) for reasoning and pre-trained knowledge in multi-modal reasoning focus on two main approaches: aligning image features with linguistic space, and converting images into textual cues to exploit the implicit reasoning capabilities of LLMs. Although they integrate visual information into the reasoning pipeline, they often treat visual perception and language reasoning as separate processes, limiting the potential for fully unified multi-modal reasoning. In this paper, we propose a novel method, Visual–Linguistic Abductive Reasoning (ViLA), inspired by human abductive reasoning processes. ViLA hypothesizes a plausible answer, generates the corresponding visual and textual premises, and employs fuzzy scoring to select the most coherent combination, thus deriving the final inference. This process integrates visual and linguistic modalities into interpretable abductive reasoning chains, enabling unified multi-modal reasoning. Without fine-tuning LLMs or retrieving external knowledge, ViLA improves performance by 2.31% on AOKVQA, 1.7% on OKVQA, and 1.7% on GQA over previous state-of-the-art models, while also improving interpretability and stability.

[pdf](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.344.pdf) [bib](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.344.bib)abs[Open Checklist](https://aclanthology.org/attachments/2026.findings-eacl.344.checklist.pdf)

**[FactAppeal: Identifying Epistemic Factual Appeals in News Media](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.344/)**

[Guy Mor-Lan](https://aclanthology.org/people/guy-mor-lan/)
\|
[Tamir Sheafer](https://aclanthology.org/people/tamir-sheafer/)
\|
[Shaul R. Shenhav](https://aclanthology.org/people/shaul-rafael-shenhav/)

How is a factual claim made credible? We propose the novel task of Epistemic Appeal Identification, which identifies whether and how factual statements have been anchored by external sources or evidence. To advance research on this task, we present FactAppeal, a manually annotated dataset of 3,226 English-language news sentences. Unlike prior resources that focus solely on claim detection and verification, FactAppeal identifies the nuanced epistemic structures and evidentiary basis underlying these claims and used to support them. FactAppeal contains span-level annotations which identify factual statements and mentions of sources on which they rely. Moreover, the annotations include fine-grained characteristics of factual appeals such as the type of source (e.g. Active Participant, Witness, Expert, Direct Evidence), whether it is mentioned by name, mentions of the source’s role and epistemic credentials, attribution to the source via direct or indirect quotation, and other features. We model the task with a range of encoder models and generative decoder models in the 2B-9B parameter range. Our best performing model, based on Gemma 2 9B, achieves a macro-F1 score of 0.73.

[pdf](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.345.pdf) [bib](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.345.bib)abs[Open Checklist](https://aclanthology.org/attachments/2026.findings-eacl.345.checklist.pdf)

**[Vietnamese Automatic Speech Recognition: A Revisit](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.345/)**

[Thi Vu](https://aclanthology.org/people/thi-vu/unverified/)
\|
[Linh The Nguyen](https://aclanthology.org/people/linh-the-nguyen/unverified/)
\|
[Dat Quoc Nguyen](https://aclanthology.org/people/dat-quoc-nguyen/unverified/)

Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) performance is heavily dependent on the availability of large-scale, high-quality datasets. For low-resource languages, existing open-source ASR datasets often suffer from insufficient quality and inconsistent annotation, hindering the development of robust models. To address these challenges, we propose a novel and generalizable data aggregation and preprocessing pipeline designed to construct high-quality ASR datasets from diverse, potentially noisy, open-source sources. Our pipeline incorporates rigorous processing steps to ensure data diversity, balance, and the inclusion of crucial features like word-level timestamps. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our methodology by applying it to Vietnamese, resulting in a unified, high-quality 500-hour dataset that provides a foundation for training and evaluating state-of-the-art Vietnamese ASR systems. Our project page is available at [https://github.com/qualcomm-ai-research/PhoASR](https://github.com/qualcomm-ai-research/PhoASR).

[pdf](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.346.pdf) [bib](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.346.bib)abs[Open Checklist](https://aclanthology.org/attachments/2026.findings-eacl.346.checklist.pdf)

**[MapCoder-Lite: Distilling Multi-Agent Coding into a Single Small LLM](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.346/)**

[Woongkyu Lee](https://aclanthology.org/people/woongkyu-lee/unverified/)
\|
[Junhee Cho](https://aclanthology.org/people/junhee-cho/)
\|
[Jungwook Choi](https://aclanthology.org/people/jungwook-choi/unverified/)

Large language models (LLMs) have advanced code generation from single-function tasks to competitive-programming problems, but existing multi-agent solutions either rely on costly large-scale (\> 30 B) models or collapse when downsized to small open-source models. We present MapCoder-Lite, a framework for distilling the complex reasoning of large, multi-agent coding systems into a single 7B model. Our contribution is a novel, three-pillar methodology that synergistically generates, refines, and encodes multi-agent knowledge: (i) pass-based trajectory distillation from strong LLMs fixes format fragility in retrieval and reduces failures in debugging, (ii) supervisor-guided correction with global feedback strengthens planning and coding agents, and (iii) agent-wise LoRA fine-tuning delivers memory-efficient specialisation.Comprehensive evaluation on xCodeEval, APPS, and CodeContests shows that MapCoder-Lite more than doubles xCodeEval accuracy (13.2% → 28.3%), eliminates all format failures, while reducing GPU memory and token-generation time by 4× compared to a 32B model. It also achieves over 10% gains on simpler coding benchmarks, demonstrating broad improvements beyond competitive programming. These results demonstrate that careful agent-wise fine-tuning unleashes high-quality multi-agent coding on a small language model. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/aiha-lab/MapCoder-Lite.

**[When Do Language Models Endorse Limitations on Human Rights Principles?](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.347/)**

[Keenan Samway](https://aclanthology.org/people/keenan-samway/)
\|
[Miu Nicole Takagi](https://aclanthology.org/people/miu-nicole-takagi/unverified/)
\|
[Rada Mihalcea](https://aclanthology.org/people/rada-mihalcea/)
\|
[Bernhard Schölkopf](https://aclanthology.org/people/bernhard-scholkopf/)
\|
[Ilias Chalkidis](https://aclanthology.org/people/ilias-chalkidis/)
\|
[Daniel Hershcovich](https://aclanthology.org/people/daniel-hershcovich/)
\|
[Zhijing Jin](https://aclanthology.org/people/zhijing-jin/)

As Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly mediate global information access with the potential to shape public discourse, their alignment with universal human rights principles becomes important to ensure that these rights are abided by in high stakes AI-mediated interactions. In this paper, we evaluate how LLMs navigate trade-offs involving the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), leveraging 1,152 synthetically generated scenarios across 24 rights articles and eight languages. Our analysis of eleven major LLMs reveals systematic biases where models: (1) accept limiting Economic, Social, and Cultural rights more often than Political and Civil rights, (2) demonstrate significant cross-linguistic variation with elevated endorsement rates of rights-limiting actions in Chinese and Hindi compared to English or Romanian, (3) show substantial susceptibility to prompt-based steering, and (4) exhibit noticeable differences between Likert and open-ended responses, highlighting critical challenges in LLM preference assessment.

**[Active Learning with Non-Uniform Costs for African Natural Language Processing](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.349/)**

[Bonaventure F. P. Dossou](https://aclanthology.org/people/bonaventure-f-p-dossou/unverified/)
\|
[Ines Arous](https://aclanthology.org/people/ines-arous/)
\|
[Audrey Durand](https://aclanthology.org/people/audrey-durand/unverified/)
\|
[Jackie Chi Kit Cheung](https://aclanthology.org/people/jackie-chi-kit-cheung/)

Labeling datasets for African languages poses substantial challenges due to the diverse settings in which annotations are collected, leading to highly variable labeling costs. These costs vary with task complexity, annotator expertise, and data availability. Yet, most active learning (AL) frameworks assume uniform annotation costs, limiting their applicability in real-world, resource-constrained scenarios. To address this, we introduce KnapsackBALD, a novel cost-aware active learning method that integrates the BatchBALD acquisition strategy with a 0-1 Knapsack optimization objective to select informative and budget-efficient samples. We evaluate KnapsackBALD on the MasakhaNEWS dataset, a multilingual news classification benchmark covering 11 African languages. Our method consistently outperforms seven strong active learning baselines, including BALD, BatchBALD, and stochastic sampling variants such as PowerBALD and Softmax-BALD, across all three cost scenarios. The performance gap widens as annotation cost imbalances become more extreme, demonstrating the robustness of KnapsackBALD in different cost settings. These findings show that when annotation costs are explicitly heterogeneous, cost-sensitive acquisition is critical for effective active learning, as demonstrated in African Languages NLP and similar settings. Our code base is open-sourced here.

[pdf](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.350.pdf) [bib](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.350.bib)abs[Open Checklist](https://aclanthology.org/attachments/2026.findings-eacl.350.checklist.pdf)

**[CrisiText: A dataset of warning messages for LLM training in emergency communication](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.350/)**

[Giacomo Gonella](https://aclanthology.org/people/giacomo-gonella/unverified/)
\|
[Gian Maria Campedelli](https://aclanthology.org/people/gian-maria-campedelli/)
\|
[Stefano Menini](https://aclanthology.org/people/stefano-menini/)
\|
[Marco Guerini](https://aclanthology.org/people/marco-guerini/)

Effectively identifying threats and mitigating their potential damage during crisis situations, such as natural disasters or violent attacks, is paramount for safeguarding endangered individuals. To tackle these challenges, AI has been used to assist humans in emergency situations. Still, the use of NLP techniques remains limited and mostly focuses on classification tasks. The significant potential of timely warning message generation using NLG architectures, however, has been largely overlooked. In this paper, we present \*CrisiText\*, the first large-scale dataset for the generation of warning messages across 13 different types of crisis scenarios. The dataset contains more than 400,000 warning messages (spanning almost 18,000 crisis situations) aimed at assisting civilians during and after such events. To generate the dataset, we started from existing crisis descriptions and created chains of events related to the scenarios. Each event was then paired with a warning message. The generations follow expert’s written guidelines to ensure correct terminology and factuality of their suggestions. Additionally, each message is accompanied by three suboptimal variants to allow for the study of different NLG approaches. To this end, we conducted a series of experiments comparing supervised fine-tuning setups with preference alignment, zero-shot, and few-shot approaches. We further assessed model performance in out-of-distribution scenarios and evaluated the effectiveness of an automatic post-editor.

[pdf](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.351.pdf) [bib](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.351.bib)abs[Open Checklist](https://aclanthology.org/attachments/2026.findings-eacl.351.checklist.pdf)

**[Training-Free Text Emotion Tagging via LLM-Based Best-Worst Scaling](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.351/)**

[Lukas Christ](https://aclanthology.org/people/lukas-christ/)
\|
[Shahin Amiriparian](https://aclanthology.org/people/shahin-amiriparian/)

Large Language Models (LLMs) have been frequently used as automatic annotators for tasks such as Text Emotion Recognition (TER). We consider a scenario in which annotators assign at least one emotion label from a large set of options to a text snippet. For this emotion tagging task, we propose a novel zero-shot algorithm that leverages Best-Worst Scaling (BWS), prompting the LLM to choose the least and most suitable emotions for a given text from several label subsets. The LLM’s choices can be represented by a graph linking labels via worse-than relations. Random walks on this graph yield the final score for each label. We compare our algorithm with naive prompting approaches as well as an established BWS-based method. Extensive experiments demonstrate the suitability of the method. It proves to compare favorably to the benchmarks in terms of both accuracy and calibration with respect to human annotations. Moreover, our algorithm’s automatic annotations are shown to be suitable for finetuning lightweight emotion classification models. The proposed method consumes considerably fewer computational resources than the established BWS approach.

**[Scaling Cultural Resources for Improving Generative Models](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.352/)**

[Hayk Stepanyan](https://aclanthology.org/people/hayk-stepanyan/unverified/)
\|
[Aishwarya Verma](https://aclanthology.org/people/aishwarya-verma/unverified/)
\|
[Andrew Zaldivar](https://aclanthology.org/people/andrew-zaldivar/)
\|
[Rutledge Chin Feman](https://aclanthology.org/people/rutledge-chin-feman/unverified/)
\|
[Erin MacMurray van Liemt](https://aclanthology.org/people/erin-macmurray-van-liemt/unverified/)
\|
[Charu Kalia](https://aclanthology.org/people/charu-kalia/unverified/)
\|
[Vinodkumar Prabhakaran](https://aclanthology.org/people/vinodkumar-prabhakaran/)
\|
[Sunipa Dev](https://aclanthology.org/people/sunipa-dev/unverified/)

Generative models are known to have reduced performance in different global cultural contexts and languages. While continual data updates have been known to be conducted to improve overall model performance, bolstering and evaluating this cross-cultural competence of generative AI models requires data resources to be intentionally expanded to include global contexts and languages. In this work, we construct a multi-pronged pipeline to collect and contribute culturally salient, multilingual data. We posit that such data can assess the state of the global applicability of our models and thus, in turn, help identify and improve upon cross-cultural gaps.

[pdf](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.353.pdf) [bib](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.353.bib)abs[Open Checklist](https://aclanthology.org/attachments/2026.findings-eacl.353.checklist.pdf)

**[Cards Against Contamination: TCG-Bench for Difficulty-Scalable Multilingual LLM Reasoning](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.353/)**

[Sultan AlRashed](https://aclanthology.org/people/sultan-alrashed/unverified/)
\|
[Jianghui Wang](https://aclanthology.org/people/jianghui-wang/unverified/)
\|
[Francesco Orabona](https://aclanthology.org/people/francesco-orabona/unverified/)

Benchmarks for language models have become essential tools for research. Yet, such benchmarks face a persistent contamination problem, with recent studies finding 25-50% of evaluation datasets appearing in training corpora. This is true even looking at the two-player zero-sum game setting, where most benchmarks are based on popular games, like chess, whose optimal strategies are all over the web. Such contamination hinders the possibility to differentiate memorization and reasoning skills. To rectify these problems, we introduce TCG-Bench, a benchmark based on a new two-player trading card game (TCG), similar in spirit to games like Magic: The Gathering. TCG-Bench offers three key innovations: (1) a contamination-resistant design by separating the publicly released game engine from hidden card implementations, (2) a continuous difficulty spectrum via Monte Carlo simulation that prevents benchmark saturation, and (3) a parallel implementation in English and Arabic, the first multilingual text-based game benchmark to do so. We also formalize a practical threat model and refresh protocol that preserves evaluation integrity even if specific cards leak.Our analysis across 17 models (50,000+ games) reveals that performance declines exponentially with difficulty, while model size correlates only weakly with strategic ability. We also observe cross-linguistic performance gaps between English and Arabic, with a gap of 47.4% at 32B, highlighting the need for multilingual game benchmarks that target reasoning capabilities in the target language. We host a leaderboard showcasing these results and welcome evaluation requests on our private cards.

[pdf](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.354.pdf) [bib](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.354.bib)abs[Open Checklist](https://aclanthology.org/attachments/2026.findings-eacl.354.checklist.pdf)

**[ILSIC: Corpora for Identifying Indian Legal Statutes from Queries by Laymen](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.354/)**

[Shounak Paul](https://aclanthology.org/people/shounak-paul/)
\|
[Raghav Dogra](https://aclanthology.org/people/raghav-dogra/unverified/)
\|
[Pawan Goyal](https://aclanthology.org/people/pawan-goyal/)
\|
[Saptarshi Ghosh](https://aclanthology.org/people/saptarshi-ghosh/)

Legal Statute Identification (LSI) for a given situation is one of the most fundamental tasks in Legal NLP. This task has traditionally been modeled using facts from court judgments as input queries, due to their abundance. However, in practical settings, the input queries are likely to be informal and asked by laypersons, or non-professionals. While a few laypeople LSI datasets exist, there has been little research to explore the differences between court and laypeople data for LSI. In this work, we create ILSIC, a corpus of laypeople queries covering 500+ statutes from Indian law. Additionally, the corpus also contains court case judgements to enable researchers to effectively compare between court and laypeople data for LSI. We conducted extensive experiments on our corpus, including benchmarking over the laypeople dataset using zero and few-shot inference, retrieval-augmented generation and supervised fine-tuning. We observe that models trained purely on court judgements are ineffective during test on laypeople queries, while transfer learning from court to laypeople data can be beneficial in certain scenarios. We also conducted fine-grained analyses of our results in terms of categories of queries and frequency of statutes.

# A survey on retrieval-augmentation generation (RAG) models for healthcare applications

- Original Article
- [Open access](https://www.springernature.com/gp/open-science/about/the-fundamentals-of-open-access-and-open-research)

You have full access to this [open access](https://www.springernature.com/gp/open-science/about/the-fundamentals-of-open-access-and-open-research) article

[![](https://media.springernature.com/w72/springer-static/cover-hires/journal/521?as=webp)Neural Computing and Applications](https://link.springer.com/journal/521) [Aims and scope](https://link.springer.com/journal/521/aims-and-scope) [Submit manuscript](https://www.editorialmanager.com/ncaa)

A survey on retrieval-augmentation generation (RAG) models for healthcare applications


[Download PDF](https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9.pdf)

## Abstract

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) models have become crucial in healthcare applications, significantly enhancing the relevance and reliability of AI-driven insights by combining the generative capabilities of large language models (LLMs) with retrieval-based methods. As healthcare data demand precision and accountability, RAG models address critical limitations of LLMs, such as the tendency to “hallucinate” or produce inaccurate information—by incorporating real-time retrieval from trusted medical knowledge bases and clinical literature. This dual process of retrieving and generating ensures that responses are both contextually accurate and aligned with the latest clinical evidence, making RAG models especially valuable for medical question answering, diagnostics, and treatment planning. This paper presents an in-depth exploration of RAG models and LLMs, specifically within healthcare contexts, to meet the unique demands of medical data processing and decision support. It reviews various RAG architectures, including Naive, Advanced, and Modular RAG approaches, discussing how each framework optimizes retrieval depth, response quality, and computational efficiency. By reducing errors in patient-care recommendations, RAG models play an essential role in scenarios that require high precision and accountability. Additionally, this survey addresses the ethical considerations and transparency requirements for deploying RAG models in healthcare, identifying current challenges and future directions, such as enhancing source interpretability and adapting RAG frameworks for specialized medical fields. By systematically analyzing RAG techniques, this paper provides a comprehensive guide to the state-of-the-art in RAG applications within healthcare, positioning RAG models as a transformative tool for advancing AI-supported healthcare outcomes.

## 1 Introduction

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence (AI), particularly in natural language processing, has ushered in a new era of possibilities for healthcare. Large language models (LLMs) have emerged as transformative tools, demonstrating remarkable capabilities in understanding, generating, and reasoning with medical language \[ [1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR1 "Floridi L, Chiriatti M (2020) Gpt-3: its nature, scope, limits, and consequences"), [2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR2 "Achiam J et al (2023) Gpt-4 technical report")\]. From general-purpose models like GPT-3 and GPT-4 to specialized medical models such as Med-PaLM \[ [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR3 "Singhal K et al (2023) Towards expert-level medical question answering with large language models. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2305.09617                                    ")\], these AI systems are reshaping clinical practice by enhancing diagnostic accuracy, improving patient-provider communication, and supporting personalized treatment planning.

However, the application of LLMs in healthcare presents unique challenges that demand specialized solutions. The high-stakes nature of medical decision-making, where errors can have life-altering consequences, requires an unprecedented level of accuracy and reliability. Traditional LLMs, while powerful, face several critical limitations in healthcare settings:

- **Static Knowledge Problem**: Medical knowledge evolves rapidly, with new research, clinical guidelines, and treatment protocols emerging constantly \[ [4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR4 "Lee P, Dai H, Liu T et al (2023) Clinical text summarization: adapting large language models can outperform human experts. Nat Digit Med 6:1–12")\]. LLMs trained on fixed datasets quickly become outdated, potentially providing recommendations based on superseded information.

- **Hallucination Risk**: The tendency of LLMs to generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information is particularly dangerous in healthcare contexts, where accuracy is paramount \[ [5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR5 "Ji Z, Lee N, Frieske R et al (2023) Survey of hallucination in natural language generation. ACM Comput Surv 55:1–38")\].

- **Domain-Specific Nuances**: Medical language contains specialized terminology, abbreviations, and contextual dependencies that general-purpose LLMs may misinterpret \[ [6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR6 "Johnson AE, Pollard TJ, Berkowitz SJ et al (2021) MIMIC-CXR-JPG: a large publicly available database of labeled chest radiographs. Sci Data 8:1–7")\].

- **Evidence-Based Practice**: Modern medicine requires decisions to be grounded in verifiable evidence, yet standard LLMs often lack transparent sourcing for their outputs \[ [7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR7 "Liu Y, Han T, Ma S et al (2023) Towards trustworthy AI in healthcare: a systematic review of foundation models for medical applications. J Med Internet Res 25:e47189")\].


To address these challenges, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) models have emerged as a promising solution \[ [8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR8 "Lewis P et al (2020) Retrieval-augmented generation for knowledge-intensive NLP tasks. Adv Neural Inf Process Syst 33:9459–9474")\]. RAG architectures combine the generative capabilities of LLMs with dynamic retrieval from authoritative medical knowledge bases, creating systems that can:

- Access up-to-date clinical guidelines and research findings

- Ground responses in verifiable sources

- Adapt to specialized medical subdomains

- Provide transparent rationales for clinical recommendations


The potential applications of RAG in healthcare are vast and transformative:

**Clinical Decision Support**: RAG systems can assist clinicians by retrieving relevant case studies, treatment guidelines, and drug interaction information in real-time, reducing diagnostic errors and improving treatment outcomes \[ [9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR9 "Kraljevic Z, Searle T, Shek A et al (2021) Biomedical and clinical language models for Spanish: on the benefits of domain-specific pretraining in a mid-resource scenario. JMIR Med Inform 9:e29335")\].

**Medical Education**: These models can serve as intelligent tutors, providing medical students with accurate, context-aware explanations drawn from the latest literature \[ [10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR10 "Jin D, Pan E, Oufattole N et al (2021) Disease knowledge distillation in medical dialogue generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2109.00704                                    ")\].

**Patient Engagement**: By generating personalized health information grounded in authoritative sources, RAG systems can improve health literacy and shared decision-making \[ [11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR11 "Wu C, Zhang X, Zhang Y et al (2023) PMC-llama: towards building open-source language models for medicine. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2304.14454                                    ")\].

**Administrative Efficiency**: Automating documentation tasks like clinical note generation and coding with retrieval-verified accuracy can reduce clinician burnout \[ [12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR12 "Aggarwal R, Singhal K, Tu T et al (2022) Med-palm: a large language model for medical question answering. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2212.13138                                    ")\].

This paper provides a comprehensive examination of RAG models in healthcare, analyzing their architectural variations, performance characteristics, and practical implementations. We systematically review:

1. 1.
The evolution of RAG architectures from naive to advanced modular implementations

2. 2.
Performance benchmarks across medical specialties and use cases

3. 3.
Ethical considerations and implementation challenges

4. 4.
Emerging applications and future directions


Our analysis reveals that while RAG models show tremendous promise in healthcare applications \[ [13](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR13 "Singhal K, Tu T, Gottweis J et al (2023) Large language models encode clinical knowledge. Nature 620:172–180")\], their successful implementation requires careful consideration of:

- Retrieval accuracy and relevance in specialized medical contexts

- Integration with clinical workflows and electronic health record systems

- Privacy and security protections for sensitive patient data

- Regulatory compliance and liability frameworks

- Bias mitigation and equity considerations


As healthcare systems worldwide face increasing pressure to deliver high-quality, cost-effective care, RAG models offer a pathway to augment clinical expertise with AI-powered knowledge retrieval and synthesis \[ [14](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR14 "Zhao WX, Zhou K, Li J et al (2023) A survey of large language models for healthcare: from data, technology, and applications to accountability and ethics. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2310.05694                                    ")\]. This review aims to provide researchers, clinicians, and healthcare administrators with the insights needed to harness this potential responsibly and effectively.

The subsequent sections will delve deeper into the technical architectures of medical RAG systems, their performance across various healthcare applications, the datasets and evaluation metrics used to assess them, and the ethical and practical considerations for their deployment in real-world clinical settings. Through this comprehensive analysis, aim to chart the current state of RAG in healthcare and illuminate promising directions for future research and development in this critical intersection of AI and medicine.

### 1.1 Contributions

This survey paper makes the following key contributions:

- **Systematic Taxonomy of RAG Architectures**: A hierarchical classification of Naive, Advanced, and Modular RAG frameworks with comparative analysis of their components and performance characteristics in medical applications.

- **Data-to-Model Pipeline Analysis**: Comprehensive examination of the medical AI pipeline from foundational datasets (textual, visual, and multimodal) to implemented systems, highlighting distinct challenges and solutions for different data modalities.

- **Medical Application Spectrum**: Coverage of RAG implementations across clinical domains including diagnostic support, treatment planning, medical education, and multimodal interpretation of imaging data.

- **Rigorous Model Evaluation**: Standardized assessment of RAG systems using both quantitative metrics (accuracy, BLEU, ROUGE) and qualitative frameworks (S.C.O.R.E.) across medical benchmarks.

- **Implementation Challenges**: Critical analysis of deployment barriers including data privacy, algorithmic bias, regulatory requirements, and clinical workflow integration.

- **Emerging Research Frontiers**: Identification of promising directions including real-time knowledge integration, specialty-specific architectures, and validation protocols for clinical adoption.


This work establishes a foundational reference for AI researchers, medical practitioners, and healthcare technologists developing next-generation RAG systems for medicine.

## 2 Comprehensive applications of retrieval-augmented generation in healthcare \[ [15](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR15 "Cheng M et al (2025) A survey on knowledge-oriented retrieval-augmented generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2503.10677                                    "), [16](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR16 "Sharma C (2025) Retrieval-augmented generation: a comprehensive survey of architectures, enhancements, and robustness frontiers. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2506.00054                                    "), [17](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR17 "Wang D, Zhang S (2024) Large language models in medical and healthcare fields: applications, advances, and challenges. Artif Intell Rev 57:299"), [18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR18 "Gargari OK, Habibi G (2025) Enhancing medical AI with retrieval-augmented generation: a mini narrative review. Digital health 11:20552076251337176"), [19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR19 "Gao Y et al (2023) Retrieval-augmented generation for large language models: a survey. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2312.10997                                     2")\]

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) models represent a transformative advancement in healthcare AI by synergizing the generative capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) with real-time access to authoritative medical knowledge sources. This integration addresses critical challenges such as outdated training data and the propensity for AI hallucination, ensuring that outputs are both accurate and grounded in the latest clinical evidence. The following subsections explore the diverse applications of RAG in the medical domain, highlighting its impact on clinical workflows, diagnostic precision, and patient care.

### 2.1 Enhanced clinical documentation and decision support

RAG models excel in processing and summarizing vast volumes of clinical data, including electronic health records (EHRs), research literature, and patient notes. By distilling this information into concise summaries, they empower healthcare professionals to quickly grasp complex cases. For instance, RAG-powered question-answering (QA) systems retrieve evidence-based responses from trusted repositories like PubMed or clinical guidelines, enabling clinicians to make informed decisions without manually sifting through extensive databases.

Key applications include:

- **Automated Clinical Summarization**: Generation of patient summaries by synthesizing information from multiple EHR sections, laboratory results, and imaging reports

- **Real-time Clinical Guidelines Retrieval**: Dynamic access to updated treatment protocols and best practices during patient encounters

- **Medication Reconciliation**: Cross-referencing patient medications with drug interaction databases and formulary guidelines

- **Discharge Planning**: Automated generation of discharge summaries with personalized patient instructions based on diagnosis and treatment history


This capability is particularly valuable in time-sensitive scenarios, such as emergency medicine or ICU care, where rapid access to relevant clinical information can significantly impact patient outcomes.

### 2.2 Precision in named entity recognition (NER) and clinical data extraction

In healthcare, extracting structured information from unstructured text—such as diagnoses, medications, or laboratory results—is essential for automating workflows and maintaining data quality. RAG models leverage medical ontologies and knowledge graphs to enhance NER accuracy, identifying and categorizing key clinical entities with high precision.

Advanced applications include:

- **Automated ICD-10 and CPT Coding**: Extraction and assignment of appropriate diagnostic and procedural codes from clinical notes

- **Clinical Trial Matching**: Identification of eligible patients by extracting relevant criteria from EHRs and matching against trial protocols

- **Adverse Event Detection**: Automated identification and reporting of adverse drug reactions and medical errors from clinical documentation

- **Quality Measure Calculation**: Extraction of data elements required for healthcare quality reporting and performance metrics


These systems can automatically populate EHRs with diagnostic codes or flag potential drug interactions by cross-referencing patient data with up-to-date pharmacological databases, thereby reducing administrative burdens and minimizing errors in data entry.

### 2.3 Advanced diagnostic and treatment planning

RAG models support differential diagnosis and personalized treatment planning by integrating multimodal data, including patient histories, imaging reports, genomic profiles, and population health data. The systems provide clinicians with evidence-based recommendations while maintaining transparency through source attribution.

Clinical applications encompass:

- **Differential Diagnosis Support**: Generation of ranked differential diagnoses with supporting evidence from medical literature

- **Personalized Treatment Recommendations**: Integration of patient-specific factors with current treatment guidelines and emerging therapies

- **Drug Dosing and Selection**: Optimization of medication choices based on patient characteristics, drug interactions, and efficacy data

- **Prognostic Assessment**: Risk stratification and outcome prediction using integrated clinical and research data


In oncology, for instance, a RAG-augmented LLM could retrieve the latest research on targeted therapies while correlating it with a patient’s genetic markers, tumor characteristics, and treatment history. Multi-turn dialog systems powered by RAG enable dynamic interactions, allowing clinicians to refine queries iteratively—such as exploring alternative diagnoses or comparing treatment efficacy—based on real-time retrieval of relevant studies or case reports.

### 2.4 Multimodal applications in medical imaging and pathology

RAG extends its utility to visual data interpretation, bridging the gap between imaging analysis and textual medical knowledge. These systems integrate computer vision capabilities with medical literature retrieval to provide comprehensive diagnostic support.

Key implementations include:

- **Radiological Report Generation**: Automated creation of structured radiology reports by combining image analysis with relevant diagnostic criteria and case studies

- **Pathology Image Analysis**: Correlation of histopathological findings with literature on rare diseases and diagnostic patterns

- **Clinical Photography Assessment**: Analysis of dermatological conditions, wound healing progress, and surgical outcomes

- **Medical Image Education**: Generation of educational content by linking imaging findings to relevant medical literature and case studies


In radiology, these models generate detailed descriptions of X-rays, CT scans, or MRIs by retrieving similar cases and current diagnostic criteria, aiding radiologists in detecting anomalies and reducing interpretation time. For pathology, RAG can correlate histopathology images with literature on rare diseases, improving diagnostic confidence and supporting subspecialty consultation. Multimodal QA systems further allow clinicians to submit image-based queries (e.g., “Identify this skin lesion”) and receive synthesized insights combining visual and textual evidence.

### 2.5 Clinical research and evidence synthesis

RAG models significantly enhance clinical research capabilities by automating literature reviews, protocol development, and data analysis workflows. These applications support evidence-based medicine and accelerate the translation of research findings into clinical practice.

Research applications include:

- **Systematic Literature Reviews**: Automated identification, screening, and synthesis of relevant research papers for meta-analyses

- **Clinical Protocol Development**: Generation of research protocols by integrating regulatory requirements with best practices

- **Grant Writing Support**: Assistance in literature review sections and methodology development for research proposals

- **Biomarker Discovery**: Integration of genomic, proteomic, and clinical data to identify potential therapeutic targets


### 2.6 Patient education and communication

RAG systems enhance patient-provider communication by generating personalized educational materials and facilitating patient engagement. These tools ensure that patient education is both accurate and tailored to individual health literacy levels.

Communication applications encompass:

- **Personalized Patient Education**: Generation of condition-specific educational materials adapted to patient reading level and cultural background

- **Informed Consent Support**: Creation of comprehensive yet understandable consent documents for procedures and treatments

- **Medication Counseling**: Automated generation of medication guides with drug-specific instructions and precautions

- **Symptom Checker Systems**: Interactive tools that provide evidence-based health information while encouraging appropriate care-seeking behavior


### 2.7 Population health and epidemiological surveillance

RAG models contribute to population health management by analyzing trends, identifying outbreaks, and supporting public health decision-making through integration of diverse data sources including clinical records, laboratory data, and epidemiological reports.

Public health applications include:

- **Disease Surveillance**: Real-time monitoring of disease patterns and outbreak detection through EHR analysis

- **Health Disparities Analysis**: Identification of care gaps and health inequities across different population groups

- **Preventive Care Optimization**: Analysis of screening rates and development of targeted intervention strategies

- **Health Policy Support**: Evidence synthesis for healthcare policy development and resource allocation decisions


### 2.8 Ethical and practical considerations

While RAG mitigates hallucinations and improves transparency through source citation, several challenges remain in healthcare implementation:

- **Data Privacy and Security**: Ensuring HIPAA compliance and protecting patient confidentiality in retrieval systems

- **Bias Mitigation**: Addressing potential biases in retrieved content and ensuring equitable care recommendations

- **Clinical Workflow Integration**: Seamless incorporation of RAG systems into existing EHR platforms and clinical workflows

- **Regulatory Compliance**: Meeting FDA requirements for AI/ML-based medical devices and maintaining clinical validation

- **Liability and Accountability**: Establishing clear frameworks for responsibility when AI systems contribute to clinical decisions


Future advancements may focus on real-time updates from federated knowledge bases, robust validation frameworks to align AI outputs with evolving medical standards, and the development of specialized RAG architectures optimized for specific medical specialties.

## 3 Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) architectures \[ [15](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR15 "Cheng M et al (2025) A survey on knowledge-oriented retrieval-augmented generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2503.10677                                    "), [16](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR16 "Sharma C (2025) Retrieval-augmented generation: a comprehensive survey of architectures, enhancements, and robustness frontiers. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2506.00054                                    "), [17](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR17 "Wang D, Zhang S (2024) Large language models in medical and healthcare fields: applications, advances, and challenges. Artif Intell Rev 57:299"), [18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR18 "Gargari OK, Habibi G (2025) Enhancing medical AI with retrieval-augmented generation: a mini narrative review. Digital health 11:20552076251337176"), [20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR20 "Xiong G, Jin Q, Lu Z, Zhang A (2024) Benchmarking retrieval-augmented generation for medicine. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2402.13178                                    "), [21](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9\#ref-CR21 "Li S, Stenzel L, Eickhoff C, Bahrainian SA (2024) Enhancing retrieval-augmented generation: a study of best practices. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2501.07391                                    ")\]

RAG has emerged as a critical approach for grounding large language models (LLMs) in external knowledge sources, particularly in healthcare applications where accuracy and factual grounding are paramount. This section provides a comprehensive analysis of three distinct RAG paradigms: Naive, Advanced, and Modular RAG, examining their architectural components, capabilities, and performance characteristics (Fig. [1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#Fig1)).

**Fig. 1**

![Fig. 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00521-025-11666-9/MediaObjects/521_2025_11666_Fig1_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9/figures/1)

Illustration diagram for RAG Query processing pipeline

### 3.1 Naive retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)

Naive RAG provides a foundational framework for integrating retrieval systems with large language models (LLMs), representing the simplest form of RAG without the sophistication needed to handle complex queries. This framework follows a “Retrieve-Read” methodology: first indexing relevant data, then retrieving information based on user queries, and finally generating responses using the retrieved content.

#### 3.1.1 Core architectural components

**Indexing Phase:** The process begins with cleaning and converting raw data from various sources, such as PDFs, HTML, and Word documents, into a uniform text format. This data preprocessing stage involves removing formatting artifacts, normalizing text encoding, and ensuring consistent document structure. Texts are then segmented into smaller chunks using basic splitting strategies, typically based on fixed character counts or simple sentence boundaries. These chunks are encoded as vector representations using an embedding model, creating dense numerical representations that capture semantic content. These vectors are stored in a vector database, which enables efficient retrieval in subsequent stages through similarity search operations.

**Retrieval Phase:** The retrieval process starts when a user submits a query. The system encodes this query into a vector using the same embedding model employed during the indexing phase, ensuring consistency in the vector space representation. The system then compares the query vector to the pre-encoded chunks in the database using similarity metrics, typically cosine similarity or Euclidean distance. Using these similarity computations, the system identifies and retrieves the top K chunks that are most relevant to the query, where K is a predefined parameter. These retrieved chunks serve as an extended context for the model, allowing it to generate a more contextually grounded response.

**Generation Phase:** This phase involves constructing a prompt that includes both the original query and the retrieved context. The retrieved chunks are typically concatenated with the user query in a straightforward manner, often using simple templates or delimiters. An LLM then generates a response, which can rely on either the model’s internal parametric knowledge or strictly adhere to the retrieved information. This approach also supports multi-turn interactions by incorporating conversational history, allowing the model to engage in ongoing dialog while maintaining context from previous exchanges.

#### 3.1.2 Key characteristics and limitations

The Naive RAG framework operates on basic principles without sophisticated optimization mechanisms. The chunking strategy is typically uniform and static, without consideration for semantic boundaries or content coherence. The retrieval mechanism relies purely on embedding similarity without additional ranking or filtering mechanisms. The generation process uses simple concatenation strategies without context optimization or relevance filtering.

Significant limitations exist within the Naive RAG framework. Retrieval inaccuracies can result in the selection of irrelevant chunks, which may impact the quality of generated responses due to the lack of sophisticated relevance assessment. Additionally, the generation phase can suffer from hallucinations, where the model produces information not substantiated by the retrieved content, as there are no mechanisms to ensure strict adherence to retrieved information. The fixed pipeline architecture cannot adapt to different query types or complexity levels, limiting its effectiveness across diverse use cases.

#### 3.1.3 Performance analysis

Healthcare implementations of Naive RAG have demonstrated significant improvements over foundational models alone. Geet et al. showed enhanced specificity in liver disease guidance generation using GPT−3.5-turbo with embedded clinical guidelines. Similarly, ChatENT demonstrated reduced hallucinations in otolaryngology applications compared to vanilla ChatGPT−4.0. Zakka et al.’s Almanac system, augmented with clinical knowledge vectors, provided more accurate and factual responses across 130 clinical cases evaluated by domain experts.

Quantitative improvements are substantial: GPT-4 accuracy increased from 73.44% to 79.97% on medical multiple-choice questions, while GPT−3.5 and Mixtral showed improvements from 60.69% to 71.57% and 61.42% to 69.48% respectively when augmented with external medical corpora.

#### 3.1.4 Limitations and challenges

Despite its effectiveness, Naive RAG exhibits several critical limitations. Retrieval inaccuracies can result in irrelevant chunk selection, directly impacting response quality. The generation phase remains susceptible to hallucinations where models produce information not substantiated by retrieved content. Additionally, the static retrieval mechanism lacks sophistication for handling complex queries requiring multi-faceted information integration.

### 3.2 Advanced retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)

Advanced RAG introduces sophisticated techniques to enhance the retrieval and generation process beyond the basic Naive RAG framework. This paradigm addresses the fundamental limitations of Naive RAG by incorporating multiple optimization stages throughout the retrieval and generation pipeline.

#### 3.2.1 Enhanced indexing strategies

Advanced RAG begins with improved indexing strategies that create more structured and semantically meaningful data representations. Fine-grained segmentation techniques replace the simple chunking approach of Naive RAG, utilizing semantic boundaries and content-aware splitting strategies. Sliding windows methodology enables overlapping chunks that maintain contextual continuity across segment boundaries, reducing the risk of losing critical information that spans multiple chunks.

Metadata inclusion represents a significant advancement, where chunks are enriched with contextual information such as document titles, sections, timestamps, authors, and topical categories. This metadata tagging—such as with dates, chapters, or document types—enables targeted and efficient retrieval based on both semantic similarity and contextual relevance. Advanced indexing strategies include adjusting chunk sizes dynamically based on content density and using sentence-based window retrieval, which allows for flexible data representation and facilitates more precise alignment with user needs.

#### 3.2.2 Pre-retrieval phase optimizations

In the pre-retrieval phase, Advanced RAG applies comprehensive data and query optimizations to clarify user intent and increase retrieval precision. Query rewriting techniques transform user queries into more effective search formulations, while query expansion strategies add relevant terms and synonyms to broaden the search scope without losing specificity.

Enhanced data granularity through rigorous data cleaning processes ensures higher quality indexed content. This includes removing noise, standardizing formats, and enriching content with domain-specific terminology. The pre-retrieval phase also implements query analysis mechanisms that categorize queries by type, complexity, and domain to select appropriate retrieval strategies.

#### 3.2.3 Post-retrieval refinement processes

Post-retrieval processes represent a critical innovation in Advanced RAG, refining retrieved content to ensure relevance and coherence before generation. Re-ranking mechanisms recalculate relevance scores using sophisticated algorithms that consider multiple factors including semantic similarity, contextual alignment, and domain-specific importance weights. This re-ranking process can utilize different similarity metrics, cross-encoder models, or even small specialized ranking models.

Context compression techniques remove unnecessary data while preserving critical information, addressing the challenge of fitting relevant content within LLM context windows. This involves intelligent summarization, redundancy removal, and priority-based content selection. Context fusion strategies combine information from multiple retrieved chunks in coherent ways, resolving conflicts and synthesizing complementary information.

By organizing data optimally before it reaches the language model, Advanced RAG enhances response quality, particularly for high-stakes domains like healthcare and law, where accuracy is paramount. Integration within established frameworks like LangChain and HayStack provides robust infrastructure for implementing these advanced techniques while maintaining system reliability.

#### 3.2.4 Framework integration and hallucination mitigation

The combined optimizations in Advanced RAG effectively minimize hallucination risks, where a model may generate unsupported content. This is achieved through strict content grounding mechanisms that ensure generated responses maintain fidelity to retrieved information. Citation tracking and source attribution capabilities enable verification of generated content against source materials.

Advanced RAG implementations integrate seamlessly with modern framework ecosystems, providing APIs and interfaces that support complex workflow orchestration. These frameworks enable rapid prototyping and deployment of Advanced RAG systems while ensuring scalability and maintainability. The structured approach to retrieval precision and advanced refinement makes Advanced RAG particularly suitable for applications requiring high accuracy and reliability in critical-use scenarios.

#### 3.2.5 Healthcare applications and performance

Advanced RAG implementations in healthcare demonstrate superior performance in high-stakes scenarios. Rau et al.’s context-aware radiology chatbot using LlamaIndex and GPT−3.5 provided more accurate imaging decision recommendations compared to generic chatbots and expert radiologists across 50 case files. Similarly, FraCChat achieved 57% and 83% accuracy in fracture classification using GPT−3.5-Turbo and GPT-4, respectively.

Knowledge graph integration has shown particular promise, with Soman et al. reporting a 71% improvement in LLaMA-2 performance when augmented with biomedical context from the SPOKE knowledge engine. MedRAG’s diagnostic knowledge graph integration enabled identification of subtle diagnostic differences in conditions with similar manifestations.

#### 3.2.6 Complexity-performance trade-offs

While Advanced RAG provides substantial performance improvements, it introduces increased implementation complexity and computational overhead. The multiple optimization stages require careful tuning and maintenance, with performance gains dependent on the sophistication of pre- and post-processing techniques employed (Fig. [2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#Fig2)).

### 3.3 Modular retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)

Modular RAG represents a significant advancement over previous RAG paradigms, characterized by its adaptability and versatility. It introduces a modular architecture that allows for dynamic customization of its components, providing more flexibility than the fixed “Retrieve-Read” processes of Naive and Advanced RAG. Innovations in this model include restructured modules and rearranged pipelines, which enhance sequential processing and allow integrated end-to-end training. This modularity enables the system to be tailored to specific use cases, making it particularly well-suited for complex applications that require fine-tuned retrieval and generation processes.

#### 3.3.1 Specialized module architecture

One of the key features of Modular RAG is the introduction of specialized modules that can be dynamically combined and configured based on task requirements. The Search module adapts to different scenarios by conducting direct searches across various data sources like databases, knowledge graphs, or external APIs. This module can implement multiple search strategies simultaneously, including semantic search, keyword matching, and graph traversal algorithms, allowing the system to leverage the most appropriate search method for each query component.

The Memory module leverages the LLM’s memory capabilities, creating a vast memory pool that aligns more closely with data distributions over time. This module maintains both short-term conversational context and long-term knowledge persistence, enabling the system to build upon previous interactions and maintain consistency across extended dialogs. The memory component can store user preferences, domain-specific contexts, and learned patterns from previous retrieval sessions.

The Task Adapter module optimizes RAG for specific downstream tasks by automating prompt retrieval and creating task-specific retrievers. This module analyzes the nature of incoming queries and automatically configures the retrieval and generation pipeline to match task requirements. It can switch between different retrieval strategies, adjust ranking algorithms, and modify generation templates based on the identified task type, which improves the system’s performance across a wide range of contexts.

#### 3.3.2 RAG flow and orchestration

Modular RAG offers remarkable advantages, particularly in terms of flexibility and scalability. Users can adaptively combine various modules and operators according to specific requirements of data sources and task scenarios. The architecture’s modularity allows for streamlined processes, enabling systems to effectively meet diverse user demands. The orchestration of modules and operators forms the RAG Flow, a flexible construct that can express current RAG methodologies and accommodate new research directions and applications.

Orchestration in Modular RAG systems plays a pivotal role in managing the entire retrieval and generation workflow. The incorporation of decision-making mechanisms allows for dynamic routing based on query characteristics, enabling the system to adaptively select appropriate processing pathways. This orchestration layer analyzes query complexity, domain requirements, and available resources to determine the optimal combination of modules and processing strategies. The flexibility provided by this orchestration is critical for improving the overall efficacy and user satisfaction of RAG systems, allowing them to handle a wide array of tasks while maintaining high-quality outputs.

#### 3.3.3 Advanced indexing and query processing

The architecture introduces an innovative indexing process, which is essential for optimizing information retrieval. Challenges such as incomplete content representation, inaccurate chunk similarity search, and unclear reference trajectories necessitate advanced strategies for chunk optimization and structural organization. Techniques like hierarchical indexing create multi-level representations that capture both local semantic relationships and global document structure, enabling more sophisticated retrieval strategies that can navigate complex information hierarchies.

The use of knowledge graphs enhances the retrieval process by ensuring semantic accuracy and reducing errors associated with mismatched content. Knowledge graph integration enables relationship-aware retrieval, where the system can traverse entity relationships and infer relevant connections that pure vector similarity might miss. Multi-query and sub-query strategies enable the system to efficiently manage and decompose complex queries, breaking them into manageable components that can be processed by specialized modules before synthesis into comprehensive responses.

#### 3.3.4 Novel operational patterns

In addition to new components, Modular RAG introduces novel patterns of operation that provide unprecedented flexibility in how retrieval and generation tasks are approached. The Rewrite-Retrieve-Read model refines queries using feedback loops, where initial retrieval results inform query modifications that lead to improved subsequent retrievals. This iterative refinement process continues until satisfactory results are achieved or predefined iteration limits are reached.

The Generate-Read approach relies on LLM-generated content to replace traditional retrieval in certain scenarios. This pattern generates hypothetical documents or answers that guide the retrieval process, particularly useful in zero-shot scenarios where traditional retrieval might fail due to query-document mismatch. Hybrid retrieval strategies combine keyword, semantic, and vector searches, allowing the system to handle diverse queries with greater precision by leveraging the strengths of different search methodologies.

The flexibility of these patterns makes Modular RAG more robust and adaptable, especially for handling knowledge-intensive tasks or dealing with complex, multi-turn interactions. As Modular RAG continues to develop, it stands poised to address emerging challenges in retrieval and generation, paving the way for future innovations in the field through its adaptable and extensible architecture.

#### 3.3.5 Healthcare applications and performance

Modular RAG implementations in healthcare demonstrate exceptional versatility and performance improvements. Wang, Ma, and Chen’s medical knowledge-augmented LLM achieved 11.4% to 13.2% improvement in open-medical QA tasks compared to GPT-4-Turbo without RAG. Jin et al.’s feature extraction framework combining RAG with XGBoost algorithms surpassed GPT−3.5, GPT-4, and fine-tuned LLaMA-2 in disease prediction tasks.

Multi-agent implementations show particular promise, with Lozano et al.’s Clinfo.ai system employing four specialized LLMs in a coordinated chain for literature search, relevance classification, summarization, and task-specific response generation.

#### 3.3.6 Computational considerations

While offering unparalleled flexibility and performance, Modular RAG introduces significant computational overhead due to dynamic orchestration requirements and multiple specialized modules. This presents a critical trade-off between system capability and resource efficiency that must be carefully managed in production deployments (Fig. [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#Fig3)).

A simple modular RAG workflow

## 4 Comprehensive analysis of RAG architectures

This section provides a detailed analysis of the three primary RAG architectures identified in the literature: Naive RAG, Advanced RAG, and Modular RAG. Each architecture represents an evolutionary step in addressing the limitations of RAG systems in medical applications.

### 4.1 Naive RAG

Naive RAG represents the foundational approach to retrieval-augmented generation, implementing a straightforward retrieve-then-generate paradigm.

#### 4.1.1 Core characteristics

Naive RAG systems employ a simple pipeline consisting of three sequential steps: (1) query processing, where user queries are directly used for retrieval without modification; (2) document retrieval, utilizing basic similarity matching techniques such as cosine similarity or TF-IDF; and (3) response generation, where retrieved documents are concatenated with the original query and fed to the language model. This approach maintains simplicity in both implementation and computational requirements.

#### 4.1.2 Advantages

- **Simplicity of Implementation**: The straightforward architecture requires minimal preprocessing and can be rapidly deployed with existing embedding models and vector databases.

- **Low Computational Overhead**: The single-pass retrieval mechanism minimizes computational costs, making it suitable for resource-constrained environments.

- **Interpretability**: The direct mapping between queries and retrieved documents provides clear traceability of information sources.

- **Baseline Performance**: Establishes a foundational performance benchmark that can be systematically improved upon.


#### 4.1.3 Limitations

- **Limited Retrieval Precision**: Basic similarity matching often fails to capture complex semantic relationships, particularly in specialized medical contexts where terminology precision is critical.

- **Context Window Constraints**: Fixed retrieval limits may exclude relevant information or include irrelevant content, leading to suboptimal context utilization.

- **Inability to Handle Complex Queries**: Multi-hop reasoning and complex medical scenarios requiring synthesized information from multiple sources remain challenging.

- **Hallucination Susceptibility**: Without sophisticated filtering mechanisms, irrelevant retrieved content can increase the likelihood of generating inaccurate information.


### 4.2 Advanced RAG

Advanced RAG systems introduce sophisticated mechanisms to address the limitations of naive approaches through enhanced retrieval strategies and dynamic adaptation.

#### 4.2.1 Core characteristics

Advanced RAG architectures implement several key innovations: (1) dynamic retrieval mechanisms that adaptively determine when and what to retrieve based on generation context; (2) multi-stage retrieval processes that employ coarse-to-fine filtering strategies; (3) confidence-based retrieval triggering, as demonstrated in FLARE \[ [22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR22 "Jiang Z et al (2023) Active retrieval augmented generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2305.06983                                    ")\], where low-confidence tokens initiate retrieval actions; and (4) self-reflective mechanisms, exemplified by SELF-RAG \[ [23](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR23 "Asai A, Wu Z, Wang Y, Sil A, Hajishirzi H (2023) Self-rag: learning to retrieve, generate, and critique through self-reflection. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2310.11511                                    ")\], that employ reflection tokens to assess retrieval necessity and output quality.

#### 4.2.2 Representative systems

Several advanced RAG systems demonstrate significant improvements over naive approaches. FLARE achieves superior performance through forward-looking retrieval, attaining F1-scores of 59.7% on WikiMultihopQA and 46.2% EM score on ASQA-hint. SELF-RAG demonstrates robust improvements with accuracies of 54.9% on PopQA and 72.4% on PubQA for the 7B model. Med-R2 shows 14.87% average improvement over vanilla RAG across five medical datasets, with lightweight models achieving performance gains up to 79.72%.

#### 4.2.3 Advantages

- **Adaptive Retrieval**: Dynamic retrieval mechanisms optimize information gathering based on contextual needs, improving relevance and reducing noise.

- **Enhanced Accuracy**: Self-reflective mechanisms and confidence-based retrieval significantly improve factual accuracy and reduce hallucinations.

- **Efficient Resource Utilization**: Selective retrieval based on necessity reduces computational overhead compared to exhaustive retrieval approaches.

- **Multi-hop Reasoning Capability**: Advanced systems can handle complex queries requiring information synthesis from multiple sources.

- **Quality Assessment Integration**: Built-in mechanisms for evaluating retrieval quality and output relevance enhance overall system reliability.


#### 4.2.4 Limitations

- **Increased Complexity**: Multiple retrieval stages and reflection mechanisms significantly increase system complexity and potential failure points.

- **Computational Overhead**: Dynamic retrieval and self-reflection processes require additional computational resources, particularly for reflection token generation.

- **Dependency on Threshold Tuning**: Confidence-based retrieval mechanisms require careful threshold calibration, which may not generalize across different domains or datasets.

- **Limited Domain Adaptability**: Advanced mechanisms may require domain-specific fine-tuning to achieve optimal performance in specialized medical contexts.


### 4.3 Modular RAG

Modular RAG represents the most sophisticated architectural approach, implementing specialized components optimized for specific aspects of the RAG pipeline.

#### 4.3.1 Core characteristics

Modular RAG systems decompose the RAG pipeline into specialized, interchangeable modules: (1) query processing modules that employ techniques such as query reformulation, classification, and expansion based on domain-specific taxonomies; (2) specialized retrieval modules incorporating hybrid approaches combining dense and sparse retrieval, knowledge graph integration, and multi-source retrieval strategies; (3) reranking and filtering modules that employ evidence-based scoring, credibility assessment, and relevance optimization; and (4) generation modules with specialized reasoning capabilities, including chain-of-thought prompting and evidence integration mechanisms.

#### 4.3.2 Representative systems

Modular RAG systems demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across various medical applications. MedGraphRAG achieves 8% improvement over standard RAG in medical QA and 10% in fact-checking tasks through graph-based retrieval. Omni-RAG demonstrates 8% average accuracy improvement, reaching 76.98% on MedQA through multi-source knowledge integration. MedRAG achieves superior diagnostic accuracy with 79.25% on CPDD and 88.65% on DDXPlus through knowledge graph-elicited reasoning. CLEAR achieves 90% F1-score on Stanford MOUD dataset while reducing inference time by 72% through clinical entity-augmented retrieval.

#### 4.3.3 Advantages

- **Domain Specialization**: Modular components can be optimized for specific medical domains, terminology, and reasoning patterns, achieving superior domain-specific performance.

- **Flexible Architecture**: Interchangeable modules enable system customization for different medical applications and allow for iterative improvement of individual components.

- **Multi-source Integration**: Capability to seamlessly integrate diverse knowledge sources, including clinical guidelines, research papers, knowledge graphs, and electronic health records.

- **Enhanced Reasoning**: Specialized reasoning modules, such as knowledge graph traversal and evidence-based medical reasoning, enable sophisticated clinical decision support.

- **Scalability**: Modular design facilitates horizontal scaling and enables efficient resource allocation based on specific component requirements.

- **Quality Assurance**: Dedicated modules for fact-checking, safety assessment, and credibility evaluation enhance output reliability and clinical safety.


#### 4.3.4 Limitations

- **Architectural Complexity**: The modular design significantly increases system complexity, requiring sophisticated orchestration and error handling mechanisms.

- **Integration Challenges**: Ensuring seamless communication and data flow between specialized modules requires careful interface design and potential format conversions.

- **Maintenance Overhead**: Multiple specialized components require individual maintenance, updates, and performance monitoring, increasing operational complexity.

- **Resource Requirements**: Specialized modules may require dedicated computational resources, potentially increasing overall system resource demands.

- **Development Complexity**: Building and optimizing specialized modules requires domain expertise and extensive testing across different medical scenarios.


### 4.4 Comparative analysis

The evolution from Naive to Advanced to Modular RAG represents a trade-off between simplicity and performance. While Naive RAG provides a solid foundation with minimal complexity, Advanced RAG introduces intelligent mechanisms that significantly improve performance at moderate complexity cost. Modular RAG achieves state-of-the-art performance through specialized components but requires substantial development and maintenance investment. The choice of architecture should align with specific application requirements, available resources, and performance objectives in medical contexts.

### 4.5 Implications for medical applications

The architectural choice for medical RAG systems should consider several critical factors: (1) **Clinical Safety Requirements**: Modular RAG’s comprehensive quality assurance mechanisms make it most suitable for high-stakes clinical applications; (2) **Resource Constraints**: Naive RAG may be appropriate for resource-limited healthcare settings where basic information retrieval suffices; (3) **Domain Complexity**: Advanced or Modular RAG architectures are essential for complex medical specialties requiring sophisticated reasoning; and (4) **Regulatory Compliance**: Modular RAG’s transparency and traceability features align well with medical regulatory requirements for explainable AI systems.
