Performance gains stem from improved handling of diverse note types (e.g., progress notes, imaging reports) and explicit prompting strategies, such as Chain-of-Thought reasoning and exclusion of unreliable snippets (e.g., unconfirmed echocardiogram findings). Manual evaluation confirmed the framework’s ability to identify twice as many PH cases compared to structured rules, highlighting its potential for rare disease phenotyping in real-world EHR applications.

LLama-2 Rag  \[ [90](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR90 "Agrawal AM et al (2025) Conversation ai dialog for medicare powered by finetuning and retrieval augmented generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2502.02249                                    ")\]: a comparative analysis of fine-tuning with LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) techniques for developing medical conversational AI systems. The study evaluates three models—Llama-2, GPT−3.5/4, and LSTM—on mixed-domain doctor-patient dialogs to assess their ability to provide accurate, context-aware medical advice. The primary objective is to improve healthcare accessibility, particularly for underserved populations, by leveraging advanced NLP methods to create robust, domain-specific dialog systems. The work addresses key challenges in medical AI, including factual accuracy, response coherence, and adherence to clinical guidelines.

The methodology combines Regex-based retrieval to filter relevant clinical text snippets with RAG frameworks using both GPT and Llama-2 for knowledge-augmented response generation. For fine-tuning, LoRA enables parameter-efficient adaptation of large models like Llama-2-7B and GPT−3.5-turbo, reducing computational costs while maintaining performance. Key innovations include rotary positional embeddings (RoPE) in Llama-2 for better long-context handling and hybrid retrieval strategies (dense embeddings + BM25) to improve knowledge grounding. The models are trained on a composite dataset spanning general medicine, pediatrics, and specialized care, ensuring broad coverage of medical scenarios.

Evaluation metrics include BERT Score (for semantic similarity), BLEU (fluency), and ROUGE (informativeness), alongside manual assessments of empathy and safety. Results show GPT variants achieve higher BLEU (0.45) and ROUGE (0.52) scores, indicating stronger fluency and structure, while Llama-2 excels in BERT Score (0.82), reflecting better semantic alignment with reference responses. The LSTM model underperforms (BLEU: 0.28) due to limitations in capturing long-range dependencies. Manual evaluation confirms that RAG-enhanced models provide more factually accurate and contextually relevant answers compared to fine-tuned-only versions, with GPT-4 showing particular strength in maintaining coherent dialog flow.

The findings demonstrate that combining RAG with LoRA-based fine-tuning yields optimal performance, balancing domain-specific knowledge integration with computational efficiency. This approach not only improves response quality but also addresses critical healthcare needs by enabling scalable, accurate medical dialog systems. Future directions include expanding to multilingual medical conversations and integrating real-time clinical decision support, further bridging gaps in healthcare accessibility. The work underscores the potential of hybrid LLM architectures to transform patient-provider interactions while maintaining rigorous standards for accuracy and safety.

#### 5.6.2 Advanced retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)

Some implementations of Advanced RAG in the medical domain:

_MedKgConv_ \[ [91](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR91 "Varshney D, Zafar A, Behera NK, Ekbal A (2023) Knowledge graph assisted end-to-end medical dialog generation. Artif Intell Med 139:102535")\] _and MED_ \[ [92](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR92 "Varshney D, Zafar A, Behera NK, Ekbal A (2023) Knowledge grounded medical dialogue generation using augmented graphs. Sci Rep 13:3310")\]: Have been developed for medical dialog generation, to explore the integration of commonsense knowledge graphs (KGs) with dialog systems to enhance retrieval and reasoning in medical conversations, each employing unique strategies. MedKgConv utilizes a BioBERT encoder to process conversation history and Quick-UMLS to extract relevant knowledge graphs for reasoning. The model then uses a BioBERT decoder for response generation. In contrast, MED encodes knowledge graphs alongside patient conversations with large language models (LLMs) and enhances them through medical entity annotation, following a semi-supervised approach.

Both models have demonstrated significant improvements on benchmark datasets like MedDialog and COVID datasets. MedKgConv achieved an increase of 3.3% in F1 score and 6.3% in BLEU-4, while MED outperformed BioBERT by 8.5 points on F1 and 9.3 points on BLEU-4. These results emphasize the impact of incorporating both dialog and graph-based knowledge in generating medical dialogs, showcasing how knowledge-enhanced neural models can refine the accuracy and coherence of responses in medical conversations. Despite their success, these KG approaches are typically designed for question-answering (QA) tasks. KGs provide structured information to predict answers, which may limit their flexibility across other conversational tasks.

accGPT  \[ [93](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR93 "Rau A et al (2023) A context-based chatbot surpasses radiologists and generic ChatGPT in following the ACR appropriateness guidelines. Radiology 308:e230970")\]: A context-aware chatbot designed to provide imaging recommendations based on the American College of Radiology (ACR) Appropriateness Criteria. The goal is to improve clinical decision-making by reducing inappropriate imaging studies, ensuring adherence to evidence-based guidelines, and addressing limitations of generic LLMs like outdated knowledge and hallucinations. The model leverages LlamaIndex to integrate 209 ACR documents into a vector index using OpenAI’s text-embedding-ada-002 embeddings, enabling semantic retrieval of relevant guidelines. GPT−3.5-turbo synthesizes responses by prepending the top three retrieved passages to prompts, ensuring recommendations are grounded in authoritative sources.

The evaluation compares accGPT against radiologists (junior to senior levels) and generic ChatGPT−3.5/4 on 50 synthetic clinical cases. accGPT achieves superior accuracy, with 82% of recommendations rated “usually appropriate” (vs. 64–74% for radiologists and 64–80% for generic chatbots) and 84% consistency across six runs. Manual evaluation highlights its reduced reliance on “may be appropriate” responses, reflecting higher specificity. The model also demonstrates significant efficiency gains, processing cases in 8 min at €0.18, compared to radiologists’ 50 min at €29.99. Key technical insights include the advantage of domain-specific retrieval (ACR corpus) over generic LLMs and the role of LlamaIndex in dynamic knowledge grounding. Limitations include challenges with rare non-imaging cases and the need for broader guideline coverage. The results underscore the potential of RAG-enhanced LLMs to augment radiology workflows by combining scalability with guideline compliance.

FraCChat  \[ [94](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR94 "Russe MF et al (2023) Performance of ChatGPT, human radiologists, and context-aware ChatGPT in identifying AO codes from radiology reports. Sci Rep 13:14215")\]:, a context-aware chatbot designed to classify fractures using the AO/OTA Fracture and Dislocation Classification Compendium \[ [95](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR95 "Schnake KJ, Schroeder GD, Vaccaro AR, Oner C (2017) Aospine classification systems (subaxial, thoracolumbar). J Orthop Trauma 31:S14–S23")\]. The goal is to improve clinical decision-making by automating accurate fracture coding from radiology reports, addressing challenges like variability in human interpretation and reliance on experience. The model integrates LlamaIndex with GPT−3.5-turbo and GPT-4, leveraging text-embedding-ada-002 embeddings to create a vector index from AO guidelines. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances performance by prepending the top five retrieved passages to prompts, grounding responses in domain-specific knowledge.

FraCChat-GPT-4 achieves 83% accuracy in full AO code classification, surpassing FraCChat-GPT−3.5 (57%) and generic GPT-4 (7%), though still below human radiologists (95%). Manual evaluation shows superior consistency, with 71% of FraCChat-GPT-4 answers consistently correct versus 2% for generic GPT-4. The model matches human performance in identifying bone location (97%), fracture type (97%), and group (90%), but lags in subgroup classification (79%). Time efficiency is notable, with FraCChat-GPT-4 processing cases in 5.2 s versus 50 s for humans. Key technical insights include the advantage of domain-specific RAG over generic LLMs and the scalability of LlamaIndex for dynamic knowledge updates. Limitations include challenges with complex fractures (e.g., hindfoot cases) and dependency on guideline coverage. The results highlight RAG’s potential to streamline radiology workflows while maintaining clinical relevance.

Yang, Rui and Marrese-Taylor, et al.  \[ [96](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR96 "Yang R et al (2023) Integrating UMLS knowledge into large language models for medical question answering. arXiv e-prints                    arXiv:2310.02778                                    ")\]: Introduced techniques to handle noise, such as Bio-Epidemiology-NER and UMLS-augmented LLMs, creating a reliable medical chatbot. However, some extracted medical terms were unrelated to user queries, leading to incomplete answers, By augmented LLM framework that integrates the Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) \[ [97](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR97 "Bodenreider O (2004) The unified medical language system (UMLs): integrating biomedical terminology. Nucleic Acids Res 32:D267–D270")\] to improve the accuracy and reliability of medical question-answering (QA). By retrieving definitions and relationships of medical terms from UMLS, the system enhances LLaMa2-13b-chat and ChatGPT−3.5 with structured medical knowledge, ensuring more factual and relevant responses. The framework employs medical terminology extraction, UMLS concept mapping, and contextual knowledge retrieval to guide LLMs in generating medically accurate answers, The model was tested on 104 questions from the LiveQA dataset, with automated evaluation using ROUGE Score and BERTScore. While ChatGPT−3.5 achieved higher ROUGE and BERT scores, the UMLS-augmented LLaMa2-13b-chat showed improvement in factual accuracy. A physician evaluation on 20 questions assessed responses based on Factuality, Completeness, Readability, and Relevance. The UMLS-augmented ChatGPT−3.5 outperformed the standard ChatGPT−3.5 in 40% of factuality cases and achieved better completeness (55%) and relevance (40%), though it slightly reduced readability due to increased medical detail, ChatGPT−3.5 had a 45% win rate in readability. Automated evaluations showed ChatGPT−3.5 had a ROUGE-1 score of 21.44 and BERTScore F1 of 83.14, whereas UMLS-augmented LLaMa2-13b-chat achieved a ROUGE-1 score of 19.97 and BERTScore F1 of 82.08. These results highlight the potential of RAG-based medical LLMs in providing trustworthy and explainable medical QA.

LLM-AMT  \[ [98](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR98 "Wang Y, Ma X, Chen W (2023) Augmenting black-box LLMs with medical textbooks for clinical question answering. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2309.02233                                    ")\]: Enhanced biomedical question answering by integrating authoritative medical textbooks into a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework. The goal is to address the limitations of general-purpose LLMs, which often lack specialized medical knowledge and may generate plausible but incorrect responses. The model employs a hybrid retriever (HybTextR) combining sparse and dense retrieval methods, alongside a query augmenter and knowledge self-refiner, to ensure high-quality, domain-specific information retrieval. This approach aims to improve accuracy and reduce hallucinations in medical QA tasks by leveraging structured, reliable textbook content rather than general knowledge bases like Wikipedia.

Key technical components include a query augmenter for medical terminology standardization, a hybrid retriever for precise passage retrieval, and a knowledge self-refiner to filter irrelevant or redundant information. The model uses GPT-4-Turbo as the base LLM, augmented with retrieved textbook passages to generate contextually accurate answers. Evaluations on MedQA-USMLE, MedQA-MCMLE, and MedMCQA datasets show LLM-AMT achieving 67.9% to 72.6% accuracy, outperforming GPT−3.5 by 11.6%–16.6% and even surpassing specialized models like Med-PaLM 2 by 2%–3%. Human evaluations confirm a 16% reduction in hallucinations, demonstrating improved reliability in open-ended medical QA. The success highlights the importance of domain-specific retrieval and refinement in enhancing LLM performance for clinical applications.

TrialGPT  \[ [99](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR99 "Jin Q et al (2023) Matching patients to clinical trials with large language models.                    arXiv:2307.15051                                    ")\]: framework addresses the challenge of patient-to-clinical-trial matching by leveraging large language models (LLMs) to streamline the recruitment process. The goal is to overcome the inefficiencies of manual matching, which is labor-intensive and prone to errors, by automating the retrieval, analysis, and ranking of clinical trials for individual patients. The framework focuses on enhancing scalability, accuracy, and explainability in matching patients to trials, particularly in scenarios involving large trial databases and complex eligibility criteria, and reduced patient screening times for clinical trials from hours to seconds by employing FAISS \[ [100](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR100 "Douze M et al (2024) The FAISS library (2024) arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2401.08281                                    ")\] for optimized retrieval and LangChain’s recursive chunking to preserve context.

Key technical components of TrialGPT include a hybrid retrieval system combining BM25 and MedCPT for initial trial filtering, a criterion-level matching module that generates natural language explanations and eligibility predictions, and a ranking module that aggregates results for final recommendations. The framework utilizes GPT-4 and GPT−3.5 as backbone models, employing chain-of-thought prompting to ensure interpretable outputs. Evaluations on synthetic patient cohorts demonstrate that TrialGPT achieves 87.3% accuracy in criterion-level matching, closely approximating expert performance, and reduces screening time by 42.6% in a user study. The model also outperforms baseline methods by 43.8% in ranking and excluding trials, highlighting its potential to improve efficiency and precision in clinical trial recruitment.

The success of TrialGPT underscores the value of LLMs in handling domain-specific tasks like patient-trial matching, where explainability and scalability are critical. By integrating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with LLMs, the framework provides a robust solution for automating complex medical workflows while maintaining transparency. Future work could explore fine-tuning open-source LLMs or expanding the framework to incorporate multimodal patient data for broader applicability.

Manathunga, Supun S and Illangasekara, et al.  \[ [101](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR101 "Manathunga SS, Illangasekara Y (2023) Retrieval augmented generation and representative vector summarization for large unstructured textual data in medical education. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2308.00479                                    ")\]: Aapplying (RAG) and Representative Vector Summarization (RVS) to medical education, specifically targeting challenges in processing large unstructured texts like clinical references. The docGPT model integrates a nonparametric knowledge base, using vector embeddings to enhance retrieval accuracy and reduce hallucinations in domain-specific tasks. Key medical texts such as Kumar and Clark’s Clinical Medicine and the British National Formulary serve as primary datasets, enabling precise information extraction and summarization for clinicians and students.

The motivation stems from limitations in generic Large Language Models (LLMs), which often produce inaccurate or generic responses in specialized domains. By combining OpenAI’s text-embedding-ada-002 for embeddings and FAISS for efficient similarity search, docGPT ensures contextually relevant retrievals. For summarization, k-means clustering identifies representative text chunks, while t-SNE visualizations and keyword mapping streamline content overview. This approach addresses token limits and the “lost in the middle” problem in LLMs, optimizing both extractive and abstractive summarization workflows.

The LLM benefits from RAG by dynamically accessing up-to-date external knowledge, improving response specificity and reducing reliance on parametric memory. docGPT’s pipeline—comprising OCR, recursive text splitting, and hybrid summarization—demonstrates adaptability across document types, including PDFs and scanned texts. Manual evaluations confirm superior performance over generic models like ChatGPT, with docGPT delivering more targeted answers aligned with reference materials. Visual tools like word clouds and scatter plots further enhance interpretability of document structures.

Evaluation results, though manually verified, highlight docGPT’s effectiveness in retrieval and summarization tasks. For instance, processing Kumar and Clark’s Clinical Medicine (1,508 pages) yielded 19 representative chunks within a 15,000-token limit, while the British National Formulary (1,805 pages) required only 10 chunks under 5000 tokens. The absence of quantitative metrics like ROUGE or BLEU underscores the need for future automated benchmarking, but the manual assessments validate the model’s utility in medical education and its potential for broader domain adaptation.

Ye, Cheng  \[ [102](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR102 "Ye C (2024) Exploring a learning-to-rank approach to enhance the retrieval augmented generation (rag)-based electronic medical records search engines. Inform Health 1:93–99")\]: Introduces a few-shot, user-adaptive learning-to-rank layer within a RAG-based search system tailored to clinical electronic medical records (EMRs). Designed for chart review tasks such as querying diabetes or seizure histories, the system begins from as few as ten user-labeled positive examples to learn sentence-level relevance signals and adjust term similarities via a fine-tuned biomedical embedding (e.g. BioBERT). Rather than returning full documents, it ranks all candidate EMR snippets and selects only the highest-scoring excerpts to feed into a medical LLM (e.g. Google Gemini) for response generation. That modest manual effort yields rapid personalization to a clinician’s query style and produces tightly grounded RAG contexts—supporting higher-precision responses and mitigating hallucinations.

The system combines dense retrieval over FAISS-indexed medical embeddings, a learning-to-rank model trained on relevance judgments (pairwise or listwise ranking), and a standard RAG generation stage. After training with only ten positive labels, Cheng-RAG-Rank-EMR reached Precision at 10 (±0.60) on EMR relevance metrics and outperformed baseline retrieval methods, even when baselines had access to far more label data. Learned ranking preferences generalize: on unseen EMR collections involving cancer progression diagnosis, accuracy rose dramatically from approximately 0.14 to 0.50 in RAG-based question answering. Access to just the top-ranked passages enables the LLM to synthesize responses that are both succinct and clinically relevant—supporting real-world tasks such as chart abstraction, decision-support prompts, and clinical reasoning reviews.

MMed-RAG, \[ [103](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR103 "Xia P et al (2024) MMed-RAG: versatile multimodal rag system for medical vision language models. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2410.13085                                    ")\]: A versatile multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system designed to enhance the factuality of Medical Large Vision-Language Models (Med-LVLMs). Addressing critical issues like factual hallucination and misalignment in Med-LVLMs, MMed-RAG integrates a domain-aware retrieval mechanism, adaptive context selection, and RAG-based preference fine-tuning to improve cross-modality and overall alignment with ground truth. The system dynamically selects domain-specific retrievers for medical images (e.g., radiology, pathology, ophthalmology), filters low-quality retrieved contexts using similarity scores, and employs preference optimization to balance reliance on retrieved knowledge and intrinsic model knowledge.

MMed-RAG leverages LLaVA-Med−1.5 as the backbone model, combining contrastive learning for retriever training and direct preference optimization (DPO) for fine-tuning. The domain-aware retrieval mechanism uses BiomedCLIP to classify input images and retrieve relevant knowledge, while adaptive selection mitigates noise by truncating low-similarity contexts. Preference pairs are curated to penalize over-reliance on retrieval or ignoring visual inputs, enhancing alignment. Evaluated across five medical datasets (MIMIC-CXR, IU-Xray, Harvard-FairVLMed, PMC-OA, Quilt-1 M), MMed-RAG achieves 43.8% higher factual accuracy on average, with improvements of 18.5% in medical VQA (accuracy/F1/AUC) and 69.1% in report generation (BLEU/ROUGE-L/METEOR) compared to baseline Med-LVLMs. The system outperforms decoding-based (e.g., DoLa, VCD) and RAG-based (e.g., MedDr, RULE) methods, demonstrating robustness across diverse medical modalities. Key innovations include provable alignment gains via theoretical analysis and empirical validation of reduced misalignment rates (e.g., 28.19% copy-reference rate vs. 55.08% in baselines).

Upadhyay and Rishabh and Viviani, \[ [104](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR104 "Upadhyay R, Viviani M (2025) Enhancing health information retrieval with rag by prioritizing topical relevance and factual accuracy. Discov Comput 28:27")\]: Introduced a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-driven model for Health Information Retrieval (HIR), focusing on enhancing both topical relevance and factual accuracy in retrieved medical documents. The exponential growth of online health information and the risks of misinformation motivate this work, aiming to provide non-experts with reliable, scientifically grounded health information. The proposed model leverages generative Large Language Models (LLMs) like GPT-3, Llama 3, and Falcon-40B, combined with trusted external sources such as PubMed Central (PMC), to generate contextually rich and citation-backed responses (GenText). This approach mitigates issues like factual inconsistency and hallucination in LLMs by grounding responses in verified scientific literature.

The model operates in three stages: retrieving topically relevant passages from PMC, generating GenText using LLMs, and ranking documents based on combined topicality and factual accuracy scores. Factual accuracy is assessed through stance detection using the SCIFIVE model and semantic similarity metrics. The integration of RAG with LLMs ensures responses are both relevant and factually accurate, while GenText enhances explainability by providing references.

Achieves notable retrieval accuracy, particularly in top ranked results. On the CLEF eHealth 2020 dataset, the model (specifically the LlamaRAG configuration) attains a Credibility-Aware Mean Average Precision (CAM-MAP) of 0.1079 and Credibility-Aware Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (CAM-NDCG) of 0.2146 for top-5 documents, outperforming baselines like BM25 (CAM-MAP: 0.0431) and WISE-NLI (CAM-MAP: 0.0883). For top-10 documents, LlamaRAG further improves with a CAM-MAP of 0.1532 and CAM-NDCG of 0.2702, demonstrating robust scalability. Similar trends are observed on the TREC Health Misinformation 2020 dataset, where LlamaRAG achieves CAM-MAP of 0.1222 (top-5) and 0.1602 (top-10), surpassing traditional IR models. The evaluation highlights the model’s effectiveness in combining BM25 \[ [105](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR105 "Askari A, Abolghasemi A, Pasi G, Kraaij W, Verberne S (2023) Injecting the BM25 score as text improves bert-based re-rankers, pp 66–83")\] for topical relevance with LLM-augmented factual accuracy scoring (via stance detection and cosine similarity). The integration of GenText—generated by LLMs using retrieved PMC passages—ensures explainability while maintaining high precision.

Yu He Ke1, Liyuan Jin and Kabilan Elangovan, \[ [106](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR106 "Ke YH et al (2025) Retrieval augmented generation for 10 large language models and its generalizability in assessing medical fitness. NPJ Digit Med 8:187")\]: Evaluated a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline for preoperative medical assessments, leveraging ten LLMs (GPT−3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4o, Llama2, Llama3, Gemini, Claude) to enhance accuracy and consistency in surgical fitness evaluations. The goal is to address challenges like surgery cancelations due to medical unfitness by integrating domain-specific guidelines (35 local and 23 international) into LLM responses, reducing reliance on pretrained knowledge and minimizing hallucinations. The GPT-4 RAG model with international guidelines achieves 96.4% accuracy in determining surgical fitness, outperforming human evaluators (86.6%) and non-RAG counterparts (92.9%). Key technologies include Llamaindex for hierarchical retrieval, BioBERT for medical context, and S.C.O.R.E. (Safety, Consensus, Objectivity, Reproducibility, Explainability) for qualitative evaluation.

The RAG pipeline retrieves and processes guideline chunks, using similarity-top-k = 30 to balance relevance and computational efficiency. GPT-4 generates responses with low hallucination rates (0–2.9%), while Llama 2 exhibits higher rates (12.8–48.6%). The model excels in consistency (inter-rater reliability: 0.93 vs. human 0.86) and safety (4.93/5 S.C.O.R.E. score). Manual evaluation confirms superiority in preoperative instructions (e.g., 93.0% accuracy for nurse/doctor triage). However, local guidelines underperform due to sparse textual data, highlighting the need for structured medical corpora. The framework demonstrates LLM-RAG’s potential to standardize clinical workflows, though human oversight remains critical for nuanced decisions.

Pingua, Bhagyajit and Sahoo  \[ [107](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR107 "Pingua B et al (2025) Medical LLMs: fine-tuning vs. retrieval-augmented generation. Bioengineering 12:687")\]: Explored the adaptation of large language models (LLMs) for medical question-answering by comparing fine-tuning (FT), retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and a hybrid FT+RAG approach. Five models—Llama−3.1-8B, Gemma-2-9B, Mistral-7B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B, and Phi−3.5-Mini-Instruct—are fine-tuned on the MedQuAD dataset, which contains 47,457 medical question-answer pairs covering symptoms, treatments, and diseases. The goal is to enhance LLMs’ accuracy and contextual relevance in healthcare applications, addressing the challenge of delivering reliable medical information. Techniques like 4-bit quantization, LoRA for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, and ChromaDB for retrieval augmentation are employed to optimize performance and computational efficiency.

The hybrid FT+RAG approach demonstrates superior performance, particularly for Llama−3.1-8B and Phi−3.5-Mini-Instruct, achieving higher BLEU (0.163 for RAG, 0.136 for FT+RAG) and BERTScore F1 (0.891 for FT+RAG) compared to standalone FT. RAG alone also outperforms FT in most metrics, leveraging external knowledge retrieval to improve response quality. Evaluation includes lexical metrics (BLEU, ROUGE) and semantic measures (BERTScore, SBERT cosine similarity), with Llama−3.1-8B showing the best overall results. The study highlights the trade-offs between methods, noting that RAG excels in dynamic knowledge integration, while FT+RAG combines domain adaptation with retrieval benefits. Manual evaluation is not mentioned; results are based on automated metrics, with statistical significance confirmed via bootstrapping and Wilcoxon tests.

Rationale-Guided Retrieval Augmented Generation RAG2 \[ [108](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR108 "Sohn J et al (2024) Rationale-guided retrieval augmented generation for medical question answering. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2411.00300                                    ")\]: An advanced framework designed to enhance medical question-answering (QA) by addressing key limitations of traditional RAG methods. The framework tackles three core challenges: irrelevant context retrieval, poorly targeted medical queries, and retriever bias toward dominant corpora. To achieve this, RAG2 integrates three innovations: (1) a perplexity-based filtering model (trained on Flan-T5) to selectively retain informative document snippets, (2) LLM-generated rationales (using chain-of-thought prompting) as refined queries to improve retrieval precision, and (3) balanced retrieval from four diverse biomedical corpora (PubMed, PMC, textbooks, clinical guidelines) to mitigate bias. The goal is to improve the reliability and accuracy of LLMs in high-stakes medical applications by dynamically combining parametric knowledge with curated external evidence.

RAG2 leverages state-of-the-art LLMs like Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Meerkat-7B, and GPT-4o, enhanced with retrieval from MedCPT and reranking. The framework demonstrates significant performance gains, achieving 6.1%, 3.8%, and 0.9% average accuracy improvements on MedQA, MedMCQA, and MMLU-Med benchmarks, respectively. Key results include a 91.1% accuracy on MedQA with GPT-4o and 5.6% superiority over MedRAG. Automated metrics (ROUGE-L, BERTScore) confirm semantic coherence, while ablation studies validate the necessity of rationale-guided filtering and balanced retrieval. The approach avoids costly iterative processes, relying instead on single-pass generation and a lightweight filtering model, making it computationally efficient. By refining query formulation, retrieval, and filtering, RAG2 advances the practical deployment of LLMs in clinical decision-making, ensuring responses are both contextually relevant and grounded in authoritative medical knowledge.

RAGEv  \[ [109](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR109 "Ceresa M et al (2025) Retrieval augmented generation evaluation for health documents. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2505.04680                                    ")\]: Developed to process and synthesize healthcare documents and scientific publications using a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework integrated with Large Language Models (LLMs). In the medical domain, RAGEv aims to support clinicians, policymakers, and researchers by addressing the challenge of information overload and delivering concise, trustworthy responses to domain-specific queries. The system processes a wide range of document types, including PubMed articles and policy reports, focusing on enhancing the factual consistency and usability of generated outputs in real-world decision-making and policy support.

The motivation behind RAGEv is to mitigate common issues in LLM applications for healthcare, such as hallucinations, lack of explainability, and poor factual grounding. The primary objective is to establish a reference implementation that ensures reliable, context-aware answer generation for complex medical and scientific content. To support this, the study introduces RAGEv-Bench, a benchmark suite comprising both public datasets like PubMedQA and internal use cases such as Horizon Europe project outputs, Virtual Human Twin research, and Antimicrobial Resistance reviews. These datasets enable robust performance evaluation under both automatic and manual testing scenarios, reflecting realistic use conditions.

Technically, RAGEv leverages hybrid retrieval pipelines including SHy, ColBERTv2, and full-text/vector search with reranking. The SHy pipeline demonstrates strong performance by scanning each document in isolation to maximize context coverage. Embedding models such as E5-Mistral and SFR-Embedding-Mistral are employed to encode documents and queries into high-dimensional vector space. For generation, the system uses GPT-based LLMs hosted in a secure infrastructure (GPT@JRC), which receive prompt-augmented queries grounded in retrieved document segments. Evaluation metrics include classification-based scores (accuracy, precision, recall, F1), generative scores (ROUGE, BERTScore), and manual usability assessments across question types.

Evaluation results show that integrating LLMs with RAG pipelines significantly improves both short-form (yes/no) and long-form answer quality. The SHy pipeline achieves precision of 0.85 and BERTScore F1 of 0.83 on PubMedQA, outperforming non-RAG baselines. Manual usability evaluations further confirm the system’s effectiveness, with most answers rated correct or near-complete across three use cases. The combination of document-specific retrieval and controlled generation enables the LLM to provide domain-relevant, verifiable outputs, thereby aligning with the model’s core goal of producing safe and high-quality responses in the health sector.

CliniqIR  \[ [110](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR110 "Abdullahi T, Mercurio L, Singh R, Eickhoff C (2024) Retrieval-based diagnostic decision support: mixed methods study. JMIR Med Inform 12:e50209")\]: A retrieval-based diagnostic decision support framework designed to address the challenge of diagnosing rare and complex medical conditions with limited training data. The framework leverages unstructured knowledge resources, including clinical text records, the Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) Metathesaurus, and 33 million PubMed abstracts, to classify a broad spectrum of diagnoses. The goal is to improve diagnostic accuracy for infrequently encountered conditions by combining information retrieval (IR) techniques with machine learning, reducing reliance on large labeled datasets.

CliniqIR integrates sparse (BM25) and dense (MedCPT) retrieval methods to match patient notes with relevant medical literature, generating ranked lists of potential diagnoses. The framework employs QuickUMLS for medical concept extraction and term frequency-inverse document frequency (TF-IDF) for ranking. To enhance performance, CliniqIR is combined with supervised fine-tuned ClinicalBERT in an ensemble model using reciprocal rank fusion (RRF), achieving state-of-the-art results. The model is evaluated on the DC3 dataset for rare diseases and MIMIC-III for imbalanced data, demonstrating superior performance in zero-shot settings with a mean reciprocal rank (MRR) of 0.35 for CliniqIR-BM25 compared to 0.15 for ClinicalBERT. For diagnoses with fewer than five training samples, CliniqIR outperforms ClinicalBERT by an average MRR difference of 0.10. The ensemble framework further improves diagnostic accuracy, particularly for long-tailed data distributions.

The study highlights the potential of retrieval-augmented approaches in clinical decision support, offering transparency and scalability. CliniqIR’s ability to leverage external knowledge without extensive training data addresses a critical gap in diagnosing rare conditions. However, limitations include reliance on PubMed’s coverage biases and the need for broader medical literature integration. The results underscore the synergy between IR and supervised learning, paving the way for robust diagnostic tools in low-resource settings.

Benfenati, Domenico and De Filippis  \[ [111](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR111 "Benfenati D, Filippis GM, Rinaldi AM, Russo C, Tommasino C (2024) A retrieval-augmented generation application for question-answering in nutrigenetics domain. Procedia Comput Sci 246:586–595")\]: Studied (RAG) in the nutrigenetics domain to enhance question-answering accuracy for personalized nutrition. Nutrigenetics involves complex interactions between genetic variations and dietary responses, requiring up-to-date and precise information. Traditional AI models struggle with this due to data volume and rapid advancements. The proposed RAG framework integrates GPT−3.5 and Mistral-7B with a vector database (Milvus) and embeddings model (GTE) to retrieve and contextualize scientific literature, mitigating hallucinations and reducing computational costs compared to fine-tuning.

The implementation leverages a curated dataset of nutrigenetic studies, focusing on subdomains like food intolerances and xenobiotics metabolism. GPT−3.5 and Mistral-7B generate responses augmented by retrieved abstracts, improving relevance and specificity. Manual evaluation by domain experts assesses four metrics: relevance, depth, evidence support, and accuracy. Results show RAG-enhanced responses outperform standard outputs, with GPT−3.5 achieving higher scores (e.g., accuracy improving from 4 to 5 on a 5-point scale for many queries). Mistral-7B also benefits, though variability exists. The framework demonstrates potential for reliable, real-time nutrigenetic advice but highlights challenges in retrieval precision and dataset quality. Future work may expand to multimedia data and temporal analysis for broader clinical applicability.

Markey, Nigel and El-Mansouri  \[ [112](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR112 "Markey N, El-Mansouri I, Rensonnet G, van Langen C, Meier C (2025) From rags to riches: utilizing large language models to write documents for clinical trials. Clinical Trials 10.1177/17407745251320806")\]: Investigated the use of GPT-4 for generating clinical trial protocol documents, specifically focusing on endpoints and eligibility criteria sections. Recognizing the limitations of off-the-shelf large language models in clinical applications - particularly regarding regulatory compliance and factual accuracy the research implements a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system to enhance model performance. The RAG framework integrates authoritative medical knowledge from ClinicalTrials.gov, FDA regulatory documents, and scientific literature through vector database retrieval, creating a hybrid system that combines generative capabilities with verified external knowledge.

The evaluation methodology employs a comprehensive assessment framework examining four critical dimensions: clinical thinking and logic, transparency and references, medical terminology accuracy, and content relevance. Results demonstrate significant improvements when using RAG augmentation, with clinical thinking scores increasing from 41.4% to 79.7% and transparency (previously unmeasured) reaching 78.6%, while maintaining excellent terminology accuracy (>99%) and content relevance (80%). Manual analysis of specific cases, such as tuberculosis trial protocols, confirms the system’s ability to correctly incorporate regulatory guidance that off-the-shelf models often miss. These findings suggest RAG-enhanced LLMs can substantially accelerate clinical document preparation while improving quality, though the study notes remaining challenges in ensuring inter-section consistency and the need for continued human oversight in critical medical applications.

Mashatian, Shayan and Armstrong  \[ [113](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR113 "Mashatian S et al (2024) Building trustworthy generative artificial intelligence for diabetes care and limb preservation: a medical knowledge extraction case. J Diabetes Sci Technol.                    https://doi.org/10.1177/19322968241253568                                    ")\]: Presented a (RAG) model designed to provide accurate and comprehensible medical knowledge about diabetes and diabetic foot care for laypersons with an eighth-grade literacy level. The primary goal is to address the risks of incorrect information generated by general large language models (LLMs) by leveraging a validated knowledge base. The model aims to improve health literacy, support self-management, and reduce the administrative burden on physicians, ultimately preventing limb loss in diabetic patients. The study emphasizes the importance of trustworthy AI in delivering reliable medical information to the public.

The RAG model combines GPT-4 with a vector database (Pinecone) to retrieve and generate responses based on a curated knowledge base derived from the NIH National Standards for Diabetes Self-Management Education. The dataset includes 295 articles selected using 58 keywords, and the model was tested against 175 questions. Few-shot learning prompts were employed to enhance answer quality, ensuring simplicity and accuracy. The validation process involved expert reviews focusing on factuality, comprehension, reasoning, potential harm, and bias. The model demonstrated significant improvements when few-shot learning was applied, resolving issues related to factuality and comprehension errors.

Evaluation results showed the model achieved 98% accuracy, recall, and F1-score, with 99% precision and specificity after adjustments for ambiguous questions. Manual evaluation by medical experts confirmed the model’s ability to provide user-friendly and accurate answers. The study highlights the effectiveness of combining RAG architecture with few-shot learning to address the limitations of general LLMs in the medical domain. Future work includes exploring optimal content volume, linguistic nuances, and integrating wearable devices for personalized responses. The findings underscore the potential of RAG models to enhance patient education and outcomes in diabetes care.

Painter, Jeffery L and Chalamalasetti  \[ [114](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR114 "Painter JL, Chalamalasetti VR, Kassekert R, Bate A (2024) Automating pharmacovigilance evidence generation: using large language models to produce context-aware structured query language. JAMIA Open.                    https://doi.org/10.1093/jamiaopen/ooaf003                                    ")\]: Presented an LLM-based approach to automate pharmacovigilance data retrieval by converting natural language queries into accurate SQL commands. The work specifically targets the challenges of querying complex drug safety databases containing over 500 tables and 1.3 billion records, where traditional methods require specialized technical expertise. The primary goal involves enabling non-technical medical professionals to directly access critical safety data while maintaining high accuracy standards required in regulated healthcare environments.

The implementation utilizes GPT-4 within a RAG framework enhanced with a domain-specific business context document. Key technical components include OpenAI’s text-embedding-ada-002 for vector retrieval, LangChain for pipeline orchestration, and a phased evaluation methodology. The business context document provides crucial domain knowledge about table relationships, query patterns, and regulatory requirements, significantly improving the system’s ability to generate syntactically correct and semantically appropriate SQL queries. This approach demonstrates how structured domain knowledge can be effectively integrated with general-purpose LLMs for specialized medical applications.

Manual evaluation across three experimental phases shows substantial performance improvements when incorporating contextual knowledge. Initial tests using only database schema achieved 8.3% accuracy, while adding the business context document boosted accuracy to 78.3%, with 85% success for low-to-medium complexity queries. The evaluation methodology categorized results as pass/fail/partial-pass, with statistical analysis confirming the significance of context augmentation (p=0.0006). These results highlight the potential of context-enhanced LLMs to bridge the gap between natural language interfaces and complex medical databases while maintaining the precision required in pharmacovigilance applications.

Caregiving Language Model (CaLM)  \[ [115](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR115 "Parmanto B et al (2024) A reliable and accessible caregiving language model (CALM) to support tools for caregivers: development and evaluation study. JMIR Format Res 8:e54633")\]: A (RAG) and fine-tuned foundation models (FMs) to provide reliable, domain-specific support for caregivers of individuals with Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD). The goal is to address the lack of accessible, accurate information for untrained family caregivers by mitigating hallucinations in large language models (LLMs) through a curated knowledge base and fine-tuning. The study emphasizes improving health literacy and reducing caregiver stress by delivering contextually grounded, verifiable answers.

The CaLM integrates RAG with two small FMs (LLaMA 2 7B and Falcon 7B) and benchmarks against GPT−3.5. Key technical components include a caregiving knowledge base (8,568 documents chunked into 196,926 segments), BAAI general embedding (BGE) for retrieval, and Chroma DB for vector storage. Fine-tuning employs LoRA and QLoRA techniques to adapt FMs to caregiving-specific Q-A pairs (581 curated examples), ensuring parameter efficiency. The RAG framework appends retrieved context to user queries, enhancing answer accuracy and enabling reference citations—critical for trust in medical applications.

Evaluation on 66 test Q-A pairs demonstrates that RAG + fine-tuned LLaMA 2 7B outperforms vanilla GPT−3.5 across metrics: BLEU (0.45), ROUGE (0.52), CHR-F (85.1), and BERT score (0.91). Notably, the fine-tuned model provides references for 100% of answers (80% accuracy), surpassing GPT−3.5 (70% coverage, 62% accuracy). Manual validation confirms the small FM’s superiority in reliability and accessibility, achieving 85% accuracy for low-to-medium complexity queries. These results highlight the potential of domain-adapted RAG systems to democratize high-quality caregiving support while minimizing computational costs.

GuideGPT  \[ [116](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR116 "Steybe D et al (2025) Evaluation of a context-aware chatbot using retrieval-augmented generation for answering clinical questions on medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw. J Cranio-Maxillofac Surg 53:355–360")\]: A context-aware chatbot using GPT-4 and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to provide evidence-based answers on medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ). The goal is to address limitations of generic large language models (LLMs) like hallucination and outdated knowledge by grounding responses in 449 scientific publications from clinical guidelines. The approach aims to enhance clinical decision-making with traceable, up-to-date information while reducing manual guideline development workloads.

Key technical components include Llama-Index for document processing, OpenAI’s text-embedding-ada-002 for semantic retrieval, and a custom prompt template enforcing clinical guideline structure. The RAG pipeline retrieves top 20 relevant sentences with surrounding context, while GPT-4 (gpt-4–0613, temperature=0.4) generates concise answers and explanations. Metadata linking to source PDFs enables verification, addressing transparency gaps in generic LLMs.

Manual evaluation by 10 MRONJ experts on 30 questions shows GuideGPT significantly outperforms PureGPT (generic GPT-4) in content (p=0.006), scientific explanation (p=0.032), and agreement (p=0.008), with 80.67% vs. 69.67% high-content ratings. Language quality remains comparable (p=0.407). The study demonstrates RAG’s ability to improve LLM reliability in specialized medical domains while maintaining accessibility through open-source code and dynamic knowledge updates.

#### 5.6.3 Modular retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)

Representative implementations of Advanced RAG in the medical domain:

Med-R2 \[ [117](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR117 "Lu K et al (2025)                                                                               $$\text{Med-} r^2$$                                                               Med-                                                r                         2                                                                                : Crafting trustworthy LLM physicians through retrieval and reasoning of evidence-based medicine. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2501.11885                                    ")\]: Designed to enhance Large Language Models (LLMs) in medical scenarios through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) aligned with Evidence-Based Medicine (EBM) principles. The framework addresses key challenges in medical applications, including inefficient knowledge acquisition, limited retrieval precision, and low effectiveness in answer extraction. Med-R2 integrates a comprehensive external knowledge base and employs a multi-stage process: (1) query formulation, where queries are classified and reformulated based on EBM categories; (2) evidence retrieval and reranking, which uses a coarse-to-fine strategy to filter and prioritize documents based on credibility, usefulness, and relevance; and (3) evidence application, where chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is generated to refine answers. This approach ensures the retrieval of high-quality, contextually relevant medical evidence.

Med-R2 demonstrates significant improvements over baseline methods. Experiments on five medical datasets (MedQA-USMLE, MedQA-MCMLE, MedMCQA, PubMedQA, and MMLU-Med) show that Med-R2 achieves a 14.87% average improvement over vanilla RAG and a 3.59% enhancement compared to fine-tuned models, without additional training costs. Notably, lightweight models like Qwen2.5-7B \[ [118](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR118 "Bai S et al (2025) Qwen2. 5-vl technical report. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2502.13923                                    ")\] and LLaMA3.1-8B \[ [119](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR119 "Grattafiori A et al (2024) The llama 3 herd of models. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2407.21783                                    ")\] exhibit performance gains of up to 79.72% and 77.80%, respectively, highlighting the framework’s ability to compensate for limited inherent medical knowledge. The results also reveal superior generalization in cross-dataset evaluations, outperforming fine-tuning by 7.02%, underscoring the robustness of retrieval-augmented methods in heterogeneous medical contexts.

Ablation studies further validate the contributions of Med-R2’s components, with the evidence reranker identified as the most impactful module. The framework’s scalability is also examined, showing optimal performance at context window lengths proportional to model size (e.g., 4 K for 8B models, 16 K for 32B/70B models). Limitations include potential gaps in the medical knowledge corpus and reliance on classifier accuracy. Overall, Med-R2 presents a cost-effective, scalable solution for enhancing LLMs in medical applications, combining retrieval precision with EBM-guided reasoning to improve clinical decision-making.

MedGraphRAG  \[ [120](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR120 "Wu J, Zhu J, Qi Y (2024) Medical graph rag: towards safe medical large language model via graph retrieval-augmented generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2408.04187                                    ")\]: A novel framework known as Medical GraphRAG (MedGraphRAG), which leverages a graph-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approach tailored specifically for the medical domain. MedGraphRAG enhances the performance of LLMs by integrating evidence-based responses and precise medical terminology explanations, improving both credibility and response quality. the method employs a sophisticated graph construction technique—Triple Graph Construction—to generate meaningful connections between user queries, authoritative medical sources, and foundational medical definitions. This facilitates the creation of evidence-based triples that underpin the LLM’s reasoning, ensuring traceability to credible sources while maintaining clarity in explanations. Developed a unique retrieval strategy, U-Retrieval, which streamlines the process of integrating relevant information from extensive datasets without incurring high computational costs. By organizing the graph using a hierarchical tagging system, U-Retrieval efficiently identifies and synthesizes relevant data, balancing global context awareness with retrieval efficiency. evaluation was done a cross various medical QA benchmarks demonstrate that MedGraphRAG not only surpasses traditional RAG and GraphRAG methods but also establishes new state-of-the-art performance levels in medical LLM applications. Through comprehensive testing and human evaluations, findings that MedGraphRAG generates more reliable, evidence-based responses, solidifying its potential for enhancing the integration of LLMs in clinical practice and research, was tested on 9 medical QA benchmarks, 2 health fact-checking datasets, and a long-form generation test set, demonstrating state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance. It outperformed standard RAG by 8% in medical QA and 10% in fact-checking, surpassing even GraphRAG by 5% and 8%, respectively. In direct comparison with fine-tuned medical LLMs like Med-PaLM 2 and Med-Gemini, MedGraphRAG achieved higher accuracy across all benchmarks. Human evaluation by clinicians further validated its superiority, with higher ratings in correctness, citation precision, and understandability, proving its ability to generate trustworthy, well-referenced medical responses.

FLARE(Forward-Looking Active Retrieval-Augmented Generation)  \[ [22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR22 "Jiang Z et al (2023) Active retrieval augmented generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2305.06983                                    ")\]: Designed to enhance the factual accuracy of language models by integrating a dynamic retrieval process. Unlike traditional retrieval-augmented models that retrieve information only once based on the initial input, FLARE iteratively generates a “next sentence” prediction during text generation. When the language model encounters tokens with low probability, FLARE retrieves relevant documents to improve confidence in the generated content. This approach enables FLARE to actively decide both the timing and content of retrievals based on anticipated informational needs, rather than relying on a fixed retrieval interval or static input.

FLARE employs a two-step process: it first generates a tentative next sentence and assesses token confidence. If any token exhibits low confidence, the sentence is used as a query to retrieve pertinent documents, which then guide the regeneration of that part of the text. This forward-looking technique allows FLARE to address knowledge gaps only when needed, making it a more efficient alternative to passive multi-time retrieval systems. By focusing on dynamic, content-aware retrievals, FLARE ensures that the generated responses are both relevant and supported by up-to-date information.

Evaluations on knowledge-intensive tasks, such as multi hop question answering and long-form summarization, demonstrate that FLARE outperforms existing single and multi-time retrieval models, showing superior or competitive results across all tasks. Its adaptability across diverse applications suggests that FLARE is well-suited for complex, information-rich text generation, where continuous and selective information retrieval is essential, For the WikiMultihopQA dataset, FLARE direct achieved an F1-score of 59.7%, precision of 59.1%, and recall of 62.6%, outperforming other baselines. Similarly, for the ASQA-hint dataset, FLARE showed an EM score of 46.2% and Disambig-F1 of 36.7%. Across all datasets, FLARE demonstrated superior performance in retrieval-enhanced question answering and summarization tasks.

SELF-RAG (Self-Reflective Retrieval-Augmented Generation)  \[ [23](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR23 "Asai A, Wu Z, Wang Y, Sil A, Hajishirzi H (2023) Self-rag: learning to retrieve, generate, and critique through self-reflection. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2310.11511                                    ")\]: Advances retrieval-augmented generation by introducing a self-reflective approach that adaptively retrieves relevant information and critiques its own outputs to improve factual accuracy and relevance. Unlike traditional RAG models, which retrieve a fixed number of passages and may incorporate irrelevant information, SELF-RAG employs “reflection tokens” that allow the model to decide when retrieval is necessary and assess the quality of its output at each generation step. These tokens signal the need for retrieval, relevance, and support from retrieved passages, enabling the model to dynamically adjust its retrieval actions and generate well-supported responses.

The model’s architecture includes a generator that integrates reflection tokens to signal retrieval and a critic component that assesses retrieved passages for their relevance and support. This setup enables the model to generate content in segments, each evaluated and refined based on feedback from reflection tokens. SELF-RAG’s self-assessment mechanism ensures that retrieved information is only incorporated when relevant, enhancing response quality while optimizing retrieval efficiency. Additionally, the model’s inference process allows for customization, such as adjusting retrieval frequency and imposing constraints to emphasize accuracy or fluency based on task requirements.

SELF-RAG demonstrates robust performance improvements across multiple tasks. For short-form tasks, surpasses state-of-the-art retrieval-augmented models and LLMs in knowledge-intensive tasks, such as fact verification and long-form QA. The model achieves high accuracies of 54.9% on PopQA, 66.4% on TQA, and 72.4% on PubQA, while the 13B variant scores 55.8%, 69.3%, and 74.5%, respectively. In the closed-set ARC-Challenge, accuracies reach 67.3% for the 7B model and 73.1% for the 13B model, and in biography generation, F1-Score values of 81.2 (7B) and 80.2 (13B) highlight improved factuality. Moreover, on the long-form ASQA task, citation precision and recall are 66.9/67.8 for the 7B model and 70.3/71.3 for the 13B model, indicating that SELF-RAG effectively enhances evidence-backed generation quality.

MedRAG  \[ [121](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR121 "Zhao X, Liu S, Yang S-Y, Miao C (2025) MedRAG: enhancing retrieval-augmented generation with knowledge graph-elicited reasoning for healthcare copilot. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2502.04413                                    ")\]: Designed a novel Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework for medical diagnosis by integrating knowledge graph (KG)-elicited reasoning. MedRAG addresses the limitations of heuristic-based RAG models in distinguishing between diseases with similar manifestations. The system systematically constructs a four-tier hierarchical diagnostic KG to provide accurate diagnostic reasoning and dynamically retrieves relevant Electronic Health Records (EHRs). It is evaluated on both the public DDXPlus dataset and a private chronic pain diagnostic dataset (CPDD), demonstrating improved diagnostic accuracy and specificity compared to existing RAG models. MedRAG’s framework consists of a document retriever and a KG-elicited reasoning module that enhances decision-making through structured medical knowledge. The retriever fetches relevant patient EHRs based on semantic similarity, and the reasoning module integrates diagnostic differences extracted from the KG to refine diagnosis and suggest follow-up questions. Experimental results indicate that MedRAG outperforms state-of-the-art RAG models, achieving superior performance in accuracy (79.25% on CPDD, 88.65% on DDXPlus) and specificity (L3 metric of 66.04% on CPDD, 68.01% on DDXPlus). Additionally, the system exhibits strong adaptability across multiple language models, including Mixtral-8x7B, Llama−3.1-Instruct, and GPT-4o. The findings validate MedRAG as a robust diagnostic assistant, improving reasoning-driven medical decision-making. The integration of KG significantly enhances precision by addressing diagnostic ambiguities. Future work will explore multimodal data integration, including medical imaging and physiological signals, to further refine diagnostic capabilities.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation-Based Physician Recommendation (RAGPR)  \[ [122](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR122 "Zheng Y et al (2025) Integrating retrieval-augmented generation for enhanced personalized physician recommendations in web-based medical services: model development study. Front Public Health 13:1501408")\]: A Rag model to enhance personalized physician recommendations in web-based medical services. The goal is to address scalability and accuracy limitations in manual triage systems by automating patient-physician matching. The RAGPR model leverages a dataset of 646,383 consultation records, employing embedding models (FastText, SBERT, OpenAI) for semantic clustering and LLMs (Mistral, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o) for response generation. Key technologies include Sentence-BERT for document embeddings, Chroma for vector storage, and Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) for diverse retrieval, ensuring precise and context-aware recommendations.

The RAGPR framework integrates retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to dynamically link patient queries with physician specialties. SBERT and OpenAI embeddings achieve superior clustering (F1-scores of 95% and 96%, respectively), while Mistral and GPT-4o excel in recommendation tasks (F1-scores of 94% and 95%). The model’s hybrid approach—combining semantic retrieval with LLM-generated responses—ensures accuracy and personalization. For instance, dermatological symptoms are correctly mapped to dermatologists, demonstrating clinical relevance. Manual evaluations by triage staff further validate the model, with GPT-4o receiving the highest rating (4.67/5) for recommendation appropriateness.

Evaluation results highlight the RAGPR model’s effectiveness: SBERT \[ [123](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR123 "Reimers N, Gurevych I (2019) Sentence-BERT: sentence embeddings using Siamese BERT-networks. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:1908.10084                                    ")\] and GPT-4o outperform FastText and smaller LLMs in precision (95% vs. 46%) and recall (97% vs. 90%). The framework’s modular design allows seamless integration with existing healthcare systems, though challenges like data privacy and bias mitigation persist. Future extensions could incorporate multimodal data for broader applications, such as chronic disease management. The study demonstrates that RAG-enhanced LLMs significantly improve triage efficiency, offering a scalable solution for web-based medical services.

Omni-RAG  \[ [124](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR124 "Chen Z et al (2025) Towards OMNI-RAG: comprehensive retrieval-augmented generation for large language models in medical applications. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2501.02460                                    ")\]: presented a multi-source Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework designed to improve factual consistency in medical applications. The approach introduces MedOmniKB, a structured and multi-genre medical knowledge repository, incorporating books, clinical guidelines, research papers, medical wikis, and knowledge graphs. The study further develops a Source Planning Optimization (SPO) method that enhances multi-source utilization by dynamically selecting and integrating relevant knowledge sources for each query. Experimental results show that Omni-RAG outperforms existing RAG methods, achieving higher retrieval precision and medical reasoning accuracy. Omni-RAG employs an expert LLM for query planning, generating tailored queries for different medical knowledge sources. A smaller fine-tuned model is then trained via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to align retrieval with optimal medical knowledge selection. The system is evaluated across 11 benchmark medical datasets, including MedQA, PubMedQA, SEER, and DDXPlus. It achieves a significant performance boost, with an average accuracy improvement of 8%, reaching 76.98% on MedQA, 60.20% on PubMedQA, 61.90% on SEER, and 52.40% on DDXPlus compared to traditional RAG models. The inclusion of diverse medical sources demonstrates the advantages of structured multi-source retrieval in reducing hallucinations and improving response reliability.

CLEAR  \[ [125](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR125 "Lopez I et al (2025) Clinical entity augmented retrieval for clinical information extraction. NPJ Digit Med 8:45")\]: Introduced A Clinical Entity Augmented Retrieval for Information Extraction in Electronic Health Records (CLEAR), a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline that improves clinical information extraction by leveraging named entity recognition (NER) for retrieval. Unlike conventional embedding-based RAG methods, CLEAR prioritizes retrieval based on clinical entities, enhancing efficiency and accuracy. The system was evaluated on 20,000 electronic health records (EHRs) across two datasets: Stanford Medication for Opioid Use Disorder (MOUD) and CheXpert \[ [126](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR126 "Wollek A et al (2024) German chexpert chest x-ray radiology report labeler. Fortschr Röntgenstr 196:956–965")\]. Results demonstrate CLEAR’s superiority over chunk embedding and full-note retrieval methods, achieving higher F1 scores and significantly reducing inference time and token usage. utilizes zero-shot NER with Flan-T5-XXL to identify clinical entities, followed by an entity filtering step using Bio+ClinicalBERT and GPT-4. The selected entities are then expanded using ontologies (UMLS) and LLM-generated synonyms before retrieving relevant text chunks from EHRs. The retrieved contexts are processed by large language models, including GPT-4, Mixtral, and Llama-3. Evaluation metrics include precision, recall, and F1-score, with CLEAR achieving an average F1-score of 90% on the Stanford MOUD dataset and 0.96 on CheXpert. CLEAR also reduces input tokens by 70%, model queries by 66%, and inference time by 72% compared to embedding-based RAG.

MS-RAG  \[ [127](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR127 "Hsu H-L et al (2025) Medplan: a two-stage rag-based system for personalized medical plan generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2503.17900                                    ")\]: Introduced a Multi-Stage Retrieval-Augmented Generation (MS-RAG) framework that enhances retrieval precision and factual consistency by integrating dense and sparse retrieval with reinforcement learning optimization. Unlike conventional embedding-based RAG models, MS-RAG employs a hybrid retrieval pipeline that dynamically selects relevant context using neural retrievers and BM25. The system was evaluated on benchmark datasets, including Natural Questions (NQ) and TriviaQA, demonstrating superior retrieval precision and response coherence. MS-RAG achieves a 7.2% improvement in retrieval precision and a 5.8% increase in factual consistency scores, outperforming baseline RAG implementations.

MS-RAG utilizes a fine-tuned transformer model for generation, incorporating reinforcement learning to optimize retrieval-generation alignment. The retrieved contexts are processed by GPT-4 and Mixtral, ensuring high-fidelity responses with reduced hallucinations. Evaluation metrics include BLEU, ROUGE-L, and factual accuracy, with MS-RAG achieving a 4.1% gain in ROUGE-L and a 15% reduction in hallucinations compared to standard RAG models. Additionally, the framework reduces token usage and inference time, making it computationally efficient.

MS-RAG offers a flexible and scalable architecture that dynamically optimizes retrieval necessity and integrates reinforcement learning for improved retrieval precision and response quality. These advances contribute to a more reliable and efficient RAG system, with potential applications in high-stakes domains such as legal and medical text generation.

Alkhalaf, Mohammad and Yu, Ping and Yin  \[ [128](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR128 "Alkhalaf M, Yu P, Yin M, Deng C (2024) Applying generative AI with retrieval augmented generation to summarize and extract key clinical information from electronic health records. J Biomed Inform 156:104662")\]: Applied Llama 2 (13B) with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to process clinical data from Australian Residential Aged Care Facilities, focusing on summarizing EHRs and extracting malnutrition risk factors. The methodology combines zero-shot prompting with RAG techniques using sentence-transformers for embeddings and Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) retrieval to enhance information extraction from 1424 progress notes for summarization and 1399 notes for risk factor analysis. The approach specifically addresses challenges in handling unstructured medical data while mitigating common LLM issues like hallucinations and token limitations.

Results demonstrate RAG’s significant impact on structured summarization, improving accuracy from 93.25% to 99.25% by effectively integrating external knowledge and reducing hallucinations. However, for malnutrition risk factor extraction from clinical notes, RAG provided no accuracy improvement, maintaining 90% performance and suggesting task-dependent effectiveness. The system successfully optimized token usage in summarization tasks while highlighting persistent challenges in extracting implicitly stated information from clinical narratives.

The findings underscore RAG’s potential for clinical applications, particularly in structured data extraction tasks, while revealing limitations in processing more subjective clinical information. The study provides valuable insights into implementing open-source LLMs in healthcare settings where data privacy is paramount, though it indicates the need for further refinement of retrieval strategies for complex clinical inference tasks. These results contribute to understanding how retrieval augmentation can enhance different types of clinical NLP applications.

Das, Sudeshna and Ge  \[ [129](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR129 "Das S et al (2025) Two-layer retrieval-augmented generation framework for low-resource medical question answering using reddit data: proof-of-concept study. J Med Internet Res 27:e66220")\]: Proposed a two-layer Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework designed for low-resource medical question answering (MQA) using Reddit data. The framework addresses challenges in extracting insights from large volumes of social media posts about emerging drug use (e.g., xylazine and ketamine) by clinicians. The goal is to provide accurate, timely, and computationally efficient answers without relying on high-resource infrastructure, ensuring equitable access in constrained settings.

The framework employs a modular architecture: the first layer generates individual summaries from retrieved Reddit posts using keyword-based retrieval (BM25F ranking), while the second layer synthesizes these into a final answer. Two LLMs are evaluated—GPT-4 and the quantized Nous-Hermes-2-7B-DPO—to demonstrate feasibility in low-resource environments. Key innovations include segmentation for context window constraints and a focus on open-source models to reduce hardware dependencies. Manual evaluation by 21 domain experts assesses coverage, coherence, relevance, length, and hallucination. Results show no significant difference between GPT-4 and Nous-Hermes-2-7B-DPO in median scores for relevance (3/3), coherence (5/5), or hallucination (0/1), though GPT-4 produces longer responses. The Coleman-Liau Index reveals GPT-4 summaries require higher readability levels (grade 16.6 vs. 12.1 for Nous-Hermes-2-7B-DPO). The framework’s success highlights the potential of lightweight RAG systems for MQA, though limitations include data biases and narrow domain validation.

Duan, Yuchen and Zhou  \[ [130](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR130 "Duan Y et al (2025) Research on a traditional Chinese medicine case-based question-answering system integrating large language models and knowledge graphs. Front Med 11:1512329")\]: Presented a novel approach to enhancing Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) case analysis by integrating large language models (LLMs) with knowledge graphs (KGs). The study focuses on automating the extraction and structuring of key entities—such as symptoms, pathogenesis, treatment principles, and medications—from 679 clinical cases of Wang Zhongqi, a renowned TCM physician. The goal is to address challenges in TCM case studies, including unstructured data, inefficient retrieval, and complex information analysis, by leveraging modern AI techniques to improve accessibility and accuracy. The proposed system combines GLM-3-Turbo \[ [131](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR131 "Glm T et al (2024) ChatGLM: a family of large language models from GLM-130b to GLM-4 all tools. arxiv 2024. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2406.12793                                    ")\] for named entity recognition (NER) and GPT−3.5-Turbo for response generation, utilizing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to query a Neo4j-based knowledge graph.

The methodology involves four stages: schema layer construction, knowledge extraction, fusion, and storage. GLM-3-Turbo achieves high precision (0.9462), recall (0.9778), and F1-score (0.9617) in NER, while GPT−3.5-Turbo excels in RAGAS evaluation, scoring 0.9375 in faithfulness, 0.9686 in answer relevance, and 0.9500 in context recall. Manual evaluation using the SUS framework highlights superior performance in safety (2.868) and usability (2.848), demonstrating the system’s reliability and professionalism. The integration of LLMs with KGs mitigates hallucinations and enhances reasoning, providing a robust solution for TCM case retrieval and analysis. This approach not only modernizes TCM research but also sets a benchmark for interdisciplinary applications of AI in medical domains.

DR.KNOWS  \[ [132](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR132 "Gao Y et al (2025) Leveraging medical knowledge graphs into large language models for diagnosis prediction: design and application study. JMIR AI 4:e58670")\]: An advanced system integrating Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) knowledge graphs (KGs) with large language models (LLMs) to enhance diagnostic accuracy from electronic health records (EHRs). The goal is to mitigate diagnostic errors caused by information overload in EHR narratives by leveraging structured medical knowledge for context-aware reasoning. The system employs a stack graph isomorphism network (SGIN) for node embedding and an attention-based path ranker to retrieve and rank clinically relevant knowledge paths, which are then fed into LLMs like T5 and GPT−3.5-Turbo for diagnosis prediction.

Key technical innovations include hybrid retrieval combining KG traversal with LLM-based entity linking, few-shot prompting, and structured knowledge grounding to reduce hallucinations. DR.KNOWS achieves a CUI F-score of 27.78 and ROUGE-L score of 30.72 on the MIMIC-III dataset, outperforming baseline models like QuickUMLS. Human evaluation highlights improved diagnostic rationale alignment (55% agreement) and reduced abstraction errors when KG paths are integrated. The system demonstrates how KG-augmented RAG enhances LLM reliability in clinical decision-making, though limitations include dependency on embedding quality and potential KG biases. Future work may explore symbolic reasoning and bias mitigation to further refine diagnostic precision.

Retrieval-augmented Information System for Enhancement (RISE)  \[ [133](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR133 "Wang D et al (2024) Enhancement of the performance of large language models in diabetes education through retrieval-augmented generation: comparative study. J Med Internet Res 26:e58041")\]: A framework to improve the performance of large language models (LLMs) in diabetes education. The goal is to address the limitations of commercially available LLMs, such as GPT-4, Claude 2, and Google Bard, which lack specialized medical knowledge and may generate inaccurate information. By integrating RISE, the framework enhances LLMs’ ability to provide accurate, comprehensive, and understandable responses to diabetes-related inquiries, aiming to support patient self-management and alleviate pressure on medical resources. The RISE framework employs a four-step process: query rewriting, information retrieval from local and external academic sources, summarization with fact-checking and safety checks, and execution to generate final responses. Key technologies include the Facebook AI Similarity Search (FAISS) algorithm for retrieval and OpenAI’s Text-Embedding-ADA-002 for document embeddings.

The RISE framework leverages retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to dynamically augment LLMs with up-to-date medical knowledge from over 200 reputable academic websites and a local database of 600 diabetes-related articles. This approach mitigates hallucination risks and improves response quality without requiring costly model retraining. The integration of RISE significantly enhances the accuracy of base LLMs, with improvements of 7% for GPT-4, 19% for Claude 2, and 9% for Google Bard. Manual evaluations by clinicians and patients demonstrate notable gains in accuracy, comprehensiveness, and understandability, with GPT-4 achieving the highest accuracy rate of 98%.
