The framework’s fact-checking and safety modules further ensure reliability, making it a promising tool for diabetes education and patient support.

Evaluation results highlight the effectiveness of RISE, with clinician assessments showing increased comprehensiveness scores (e.g., GPT-4 improved from 4.14 to 4.69 on a 5-point scale) and patient ratings indicating better understandability (e.g., GPT-4’s score rose from 4.32 to 4.64). The study underscores the potential of RAG-enhanced LLMs in specialized medical domains, particularly for chronic disease management. However, the evaluation was limited to manual assessments, and generalizability to other medical fields remains unexplored. The RISE framework sets a foundation for future research into personalized, multilingual, and multimodal applications in healthcare.

GastroBot  \[ [134](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR134 "Zhou Q et al (2024) GastroBot: a Chinese gastrointestinal disease chatbot based on the retrieval-augmented generation. Front Med 11:1392555")\]: A Chinese-language chatbot specialized in gastrointestinal diseases, leveraging Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to enhance clinical accuracy and reduce hallucinations in medical question-answering. The goal is to address limitations of generic large language models (LLMs) in gastroenterology by integrating domain-specific knowledge from 25 clinical guidelines and 40 research articles. Key technologies include a fine-tuned GTE-base-zh embedding model (18% improved hit rate over base), LlamaIndex for RAG pipeline construction, and GPT−3.5 Turbo for response generation, with retrieval optimized via top-3 semantic chunk matching.

The RAG framework combines fine-tuned embeddings for precise retrieval with LLM-generated responses, ensuring grounded outputs. The GTE-base-zh model, fine-tuned on gastrointestinal terminology, outperforms OpenAI’s text-embedding-ada-002 by 20% in hit rate, while GPT−3.5 Turbo synthesizes retrieved chunks into clinically relevant answers. Manual evaluations and RAGAS metrics demonstrate GastroBot’s superiority over baseline models (Llama2, ChatGLM-6B, Qwen-7B), with 95% context recall, 93.73% faithfulness, and 92.28% answer relevance. Human assessments rate GastroBot highest in safety (2.87/3), usability (2.72/3), and smoothness (2.88/3).

Evaluation results highlight GastroBot’s effectiveness: the fine-tuned embedding model achieves an 89% hit rate, and RAGAS scores confirm minimal hallucination. Comparative manual assessments show GastroBot provides more precise, guideline-aligned responses than ChatGPT, particularly in complex queries (e.g., biopsy handling). Limitations include reliance on static knowledge sources and regional focus, but the modular RAG design allows for future integration of dynamic data. The study demonstrates RAG’s potential to bridge LLMs’ generalization gaps in specialized medical domains (Table [7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#Tab7)).

## 6 Comparative analysis of RAG approaches in medical literature

### 6.1 Naive RAG: foundation with clear trade-offs

Naive RAG establishes baseline performance across medical applications, revealing both strengths in structured tasks and limitations in complex reasoning:

- **Structured Medical Knowledge Excels**:
  - ChatENT \[ [85](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR85 "Long C et al (2024) Chatent: Augmented large language model for expert knowledge retrieval in otolaryngology–head and neck surgery. Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery")\]’s 58.4% error reduction in OHNS demonstrates that domain-specific knowledge bases dramatically outperform general LLMs when applied to well-defined medical specialties

  - The 26% improvement over ChatGPT in multiple-choice formats suggests naive RAG works best with categorical medical decisions rather than open-ended clinical reasoning
- **Retrieval Quality Determines Success**:
  - Xiong et al. \[ [20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR20 "Xiong G, Jin Q, Lu Z, Zhang A (2024) Benchmarking retrieval-augmented generation for medicine. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2402.13178                                    ")\]’s finding that MedCPT consistently outperforms general retrievers while PubMed yields optimal results reveals that biomedical-specific tools are essential—generic retrieval fails in medical contexts

  - The 17.9% MIRAGE improvement (71.57% final accuracy) shows naive RAG’s ceiling: substantial gains possible but still below clinical-grade requirements
- **Task Complexity Limitations**:
  - Thompson et al. \[ [89](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR89 "Thompson WE et al (2023) Large language models with retrieval-augmented generation for zero-shot disease phenotyping. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2312.06457                                    ")\]’s pulmonary hypertension detection (F1: 0.75 vs 0.62 rule-based) represents naive RAG’s sweet spot: pattern recognition in structured clinical data

  - However, the “lost-in-the-middle” phenomenon identified by Xiong et al. \[ [20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR20 "Xiong G, Jin Q, Lu Z, Zhang A (2024) Benchmarking retrieval-augmented generation for medicine. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2402.13178                                    ")\] exposes naive RAG’s inability to synthesize information from multiple sources—critical for complex diagnoses

  - Alkhalaf et al. \[ [128](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR128 "Alkhalaf M, Yu P, Yin M, Deng C (2024) Applying generative AI with retrieval augmented generation to summarize and extract key clinical information from electronic health records. J Biomed Inform 156:104662")\]’s finding that RAG improved structured summarization (93.25% to 99.25%) but provided no improvement for malnutrition risk extraction (maintained 90%) illustrates this task-dependent effectiveness

### 6.2 Advanced RAG: addressing core medical AI challenges

Advanced RAG architectures tackle the fundamental problems that naive approaches cannot solve—reasoning depth, knowledge integration, and clinical reliability:

**Knowledge Graph Integration Breakthrough**: The performance gap between MedKgConv \[ [91](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR91 "Varshney D, Zafar A, Behera NK, Ekbal A (2023) Knowledge graph assisted end-to-end medical dialog generation. Artif Intell Med 139:102535")\]’s 6.3% BLEU-4 improvement and traditional dialog systems reveals that structured medical knowledge (via BioBERT + Quick-UMLS) enables contextual understanding impossible with text retrieval alone. This addresses medicine’s core challenge: relationships between symptoms, diseases, and treatments require graph-based reasoning, not just document matching.

**Dynamic Retrieval Solves Static Knowledge Problems**: accGPT \[ [93](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR93 "Rau A et al (2023) A context-based chatbot surpasses radiologists and generic ChatGPT in following the ACR appropriateness guidelines. Radiology 308:e230970")\]’s 82% appropriateness rating versus 64–74% for human radiologists demonstrates a crucial insight: when RAG systems access comprehensive, authoritative guidelines (209 ACR documents), they can exceed human performance in rule-based medical decisions. The 84% consistency across runs versus human variability shows AI’s potential advantage in standardizing medical practice.

**Evidence-Based Medicine Integration**: LLM-AMT \[ [98](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR98 "Wang Y, Ma X, Chen W (2023) Augmenting black-box LLMs with medical textbooks for clinical question answering. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2309.02233                                    ")\] model achieving 67.9-−72.6% accuracy while outperforming GPT−3.5 by 11.6-−16.6% and even surpassing Med-PaLM 2 by 2–3% demonstrates that authoritative medical textbook integration can rival specialized medical models. The 16% reduction in hallucinations confirms that structured medical knowledge sources are superior to general knowledge bases for clinical applications.

**Multimodal Integration Addresses Real Clinical Workflows**: MMed-RAG \[ [103](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR103 "Xia P et al (2024) MMed-RAG: versatile multimodal rag system for medical vision language models. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2410.13085                                    ")\]’s 43.8% factual accuracy improvement with 69.1% enhancement in report generation tackles medicine’s multimodal reality. The system’s ability to achieve 28.19% copy-reference rate versus 55.08% in baselines suggests advanced RAG can reduce hallucinations specifically in visual medical interpretation—critical for radiology and pathology applications.

**Clinical Trial Optimization**: TrialGPT \[ [99](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR99 "Jin Q et al (2023) Matching patients to clinical trials with large language models.                    arXiv:2307.15051                                    ")\]’s achievement of 87.3% accuracy in criterion-level matching while reducing screening time by 42.6% illustrates advanced RAG’s potential for operational efficiency. The system’s ability to outperform baseline methods by 43.8% in ranking and excluding trials demonstrates how hybrid retrieval (BM25 + MedCPT) with chain-of-thought prompting can streamline complex medical workflows.

### 6.3 Modular RAG: clinical-grade specialization

Modular architectures achieve clinical deployment standards by solving domain-specific challenges that generic approaches cannot address (Table [8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#Tab8)):

**Table 8 Modular RAG clinical deployment readiness**

[Full size table](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9/tables/8)

**Evidence-Based Medicine Integration**: Med-R2 \[ [117](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR117 "Lu K et al (2025)                                                                               $$\text{Med-} r^2$$                                                               Med-                                                r                         2                                                                                : Crafting trustworthy LLM physicians through retrieval and reasoning of evidence-based medicine. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2501.11885                                    ")\]’s 14.87% improvement over vanilla RAG through EBM principles represents a paradigm shift: instead of retrieving any relevant documents, the system mimics clinical reasoning by classifying queries, ranking evidence credibility, and applying chain-of-thought reasoning. The dramatic performance gains for lightweight models (79.72% for Qwen2.5-7B, 77.80% for LLaMA3.1-8B) suggest this approach can democratize high-quality medical AI by making smaller models clinically viable.

**Knowledge Graph-Enhanced Diagnosis**: MedGraphRAG \[ [120](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR120 "Wu J, Zhu J, Qi Y (2024) Medical graph rag: towards safe medical large language model via graph retrieval-augmented generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2408.04187                                    ")\]’s 8% improvement in medical QA and 10% in fact-checking versus standard RAG demonstrates that Triple Graph Construction enables evidence-based responses impossible with document retrieval alone. The system’s state-of-the-art performance across 9 medical QA benchmarks and superior clinician ratings in correctness and citation precision suggest graph-based medical reasoning approaches clinical reliability standards.

**Multi-Source Knowledge Integration**: Omni-RAG \[ [124](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR124 "Chen Z et al (2025) Towards OMNI-RAG: comprehensive retrieval-augmented generation for large language models in medical applications. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2501.02460                                    ")\]’s 8% average improvement across 11 benchmark datasets (reaching 76.98% on MedQA) through Source Planning Optimization reveals that medical AI requires orchestrated access to diverse knowledge types—books, guidelines, papers, wikis, and knowledge graphs. The system’s structured approach to multi-genre medical knowledge addresses the fundamental challenge that no single source contains comprehensive medical information.

**Specialized Domain Mastery**: GastroBot \[ [134](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR134 "Zhou Q et al (2024) GastroBot: a Chinese gastrointestinal disease chatbot based on the retrieval-augmented generation. Front Med 11:1392555")\]’s progression from generic embeddings to fine-tuned GTE-base-zh (18% hit rate improvement, final 89% accuracy) with RAGAS scores of 95% context recall and 93.73% faithfulness illustrates the modular approach: domain-specific optimization at every layer yields clinical-grade performance. The human evaluation scores (2.87/3 safety, 2.88/3 smoothness) suggest specialized systems can achieve user acceptance for clinical deployment.

**Traditional Medicine Digitization**: Duan et al. \[ [130](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR130 "Duan Y et al (2025) Research on a traditional Chinese medicine case-based question-answering system integrating large language models and knowledge graphs. Front Med 11:1512329")\]’s TCM system achieving 0.9617 F1 in symptom extraction with 0.9375 faithfulness and 0.9686 answer relevance demonstrates modular RAG’s unique capability: digitizing traditional medical knowledge that lacks standardized vocabularies. The SUS framework scores (2.868 safety, 2.848 usability) show how modular approaches can bridge cultural and linguistic gaps in global medicine while maintaining clinical safety standards.

**Caregiver Support Systems**: CaLM \[ [115](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR115 "Parmanto B et al (2024) A reliable and accessible caregiving language model (CALM) to support tools for caregivers: development and evaluation study. JMIR Format Res 8:e54633")\]’s superior performance over GPT−3.5 (BLEU: 0.45, ROUGE: 0.52, BERT score: 0.91) with 100% reference provision (80% accuracy) versus GPT−3.5’s 70% coverage demonstrates that domain-specific fine-tuning combined with RAG can democratize medical expertise. The focus on ADRD caregiving with 8,568 curated documents shows how modular systems can address underserved medical populations.

### 6.4 Architecture selection insights: matching complexity to clinical need

The literature reveals critical decision factors for medical RAG deployment based on clinical requirements rather than technical capabilities:

**1\. Clinical Reasoning Depth Requirements**: \- \*\*Protocol-Based Care\*\*: Naive RAG suffices when medical decisions follow established guidelines (ChatENT \[ [85](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR85 "Long C et al (2024) Chatent: Augmented large language model for expert knowledge retrieval in otolaryngology–head and neck surgery. Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery")\]’s 95.7% validity in OHNS) - \*\*Differential Diagnosis\*\*: Advanced RAG required for complex reasoning (accGPT \[ [93](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR93 "Rau A et al (2023) A context-based chatbot surpasses radiologists and generic ChatGPT in following the ACR appropriateness guidelines. Radiology 308:e230970")\] outperforming radiologists in imaging recommendations) - \*\*Specialized Practice\*\*: Modular RAG essential for domain expertise (GastroBot \[ [134](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR134 "Zhou Q et al (2024) GastroBot: a Chinese gastrointestinal disease chatbot based on the retrieval-augmented generation. Front Med 11:1392555")\]’s clinical-grade gastroenterology performance)

**2\. Knowledge Integration Complexity**: Systems that require authoritative source synthesis—such as LLM-AMT \[ [98](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR98 "Wang Y, Ma X, Chen W (2023) Augmenting black-box LLMs with medical textbooks for clinical question answering. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2309.02233                                    ")\] which integrates medical textbooks and outperforms Med-PaLM 2—demonstrate that medical AI cannot rely on single knowledge bases. The consistent superior performance of biomedical-specific retrievers (MedCPT over general alternatives in Xiong et al. \[ [20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR20 "Xiong G, Jin Q, Lu Z, Zhang A (2024) Benchmarking retrieval-augmented generation for medicine. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2402.13178                                    ")\]) suggests specialized retrieval is fundamental, not optional.

**3\. Resource Optimization Insights**: The inverse relationship between model size and RAG benefits (Med-R2 \[ [117](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR117 "Lu K et al (2025)                                                                               $$\text{Med-} r^2$$                                                               Med-                                                r                         2                                                                                : Crafting trustworthy LLM physicians through retrieval and reasoning of evidence-based medicine. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2501.11885                                    ")\]’s 79.72% gains for 7B models versus diminishing returns for larger models) reveals a critical insight: resource-constrained medical settings may achieve superior outcomes with sophisticated RAG architectures plus smaller models rather than large foundation models alone.

### 6.5 Persistent clinical deployment barriers

Despite impressive technical achievements, fundamental challenges persist across all architectures:

**Human Acceptance vs. Technical Performance Gap**: The disconnect between Almanac \[ [88](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR88 "Zakka C et al (2024) Almanac–retrieval-augmented language models for clinical medicine. NEJM AI 1:AIoa2300068")\]’s 18% factual accuracy improvement and 57% physician preference for ChatGPT reveals that medical AI evaluation metrics don’t capture clinical usability factors. This pattern appears across studies where technical superiority doesn’t translate to user acceptance.

**Static Knowledge vs. Dynamic Medicine**: Multiple systems acknowledge limitations from static knowledge bases (evident in Med-R2 \[ [117](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR117 "Lu K et al (2025)                                                                               $$\text{Med-} r^2$$                                                               Med-                                                r                         2                                                                                : Crafting trustworthy LLM physicians through retrieval and reasoning of evidence-based medicine. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2501.11885                                    ")\]’s dependency on corpus quality, CliniqIR \[ [110](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR110 "Abdullahi T, Mercurio L, Singh R, Eickhoff C (2024) Retrieval-based diagnostic decision support: mixed methods study. JMIR Med Inform 12:e50209")\]’s reliance on PubMed coverage biases). The requirement for “dynamic updates” mentioned across studies indicates no current RAG architecture adequately addresses medicine’s rapid evolution.

**Specialization-Generalization Tension**: High-performing specialized systems (GastroBot \[ [134](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR134 "Zhou Q et al (2024) GastroBot: a Chinese gastrointestinal disease chatbot based on the retrieval-augmented generation. Front Med 11:1392555")\], CaLM \[ [115](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR115 "Parmanto B et al (2024) A reliable and accessible caregiving language model (CALM) to support tools for caregivers: development and evaluation study. JMIR Format Res 8:e54633")\], Duan et al. \[ [130](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR130 "Duan Y et al (2025) Research on a traditional Chinese medicine case-based question-answering system integrating large language models and knowledge graphs. Front Med 11:1512329")\]’s TCM system) achieve clinical-grade performance but lack transferability. Medical practice requires both deep specialization and broad knowledge—a fundamental tension that current modular approaches address through vertical depth but sacrifice horizontal breadth, limiting practical deployment scalability.

### 6.6 The effect of RAG on clinical models

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a critical architectural choice that directly addresses many of the deployment barriers detailed above, particularly the tension between static knowledge and dynamic medicine. By coupling Large Language Models (LLMs) with external, up-to-date knowledge bases, RAG enables models to access the latest research and clinical guidelines, mitigating issues of outdated information and improving factual accuracy (Fig. [6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#Fig6)).

## 7 Future work and challenges

### 7.1 Architectural evolution and integration challenges

#### 7.1.1 Multi-modal integration beyond text

Current RAG implementations in medicine primarily focus on textual knowledge retrieval, with limited exploration of truly integrated multi-modal approaches. While MMed-RAG \[ [103](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR103 "Xia P et al (2024) MMed-RAG: versatile multimodal rag system for medical vision language models. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2410.13085                                    ")\] demonstrates promising results with 43.8% factual accuracy improvements in medical vision-language tasks, significant gaps remain in comprehensive multi-modal medical reasoning. Future work must address the integration of medical imaging, laboratory results, genomic data, and physiological signals within unified RAG architectures. The challenge lies not merely in processing different data types, but in developing retrieval mechanisms that can identify relevant cross-modal correlations—for instance, retrieving relevant radiological images when processing textual symptom descriptions, or correlating genetic variants with phenotypic presentations from literature.

The development of unified embedding spaces that can represent relationships between textual medical knowledge, visual pathology patterns, genomic sequences, and temporal physiological data presents a fundamental research challenge. Current approaches like those demonstrated in MedGraphRAG \[ [120](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR120 "Wu J, Zhu J, Qi Y (2024) Medical graph rag: towards safe medical large language model via graph retrieval-augmented generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2408.04187                                    ")\] with Triple Graph Construction could be extended to incorporate multi-modal nodes, but the computational complexity and knowledge representation challenges scale exponentially with additional modalities.

#### 7.1.2 Real-time knowledge update mechanisms

A critical limitation identified across multiple studies is the dependency on static knowledge bases. Med-R2 \[ [117](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR117 "Lu K et al (2025)                                                                               $$\text{Med-} r^2$$                                                               Med-                                                r                         2                                                                                : Crafting trustworthy LLM physicians through retrieval and reasoning of evidence-based medicine. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2501.11885                                    ")\] acknowledges potential gaps in medical knowledge corpus, while CliniqIR \[ [110](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR110 "Abdullahi T, Mercurio L, Singh R, Eickhoff C (2024) Retrieval-based diagnostic decision support: mixed methods study. JMIR Med Inform 12:e50209")\] highlights coverage biases in PubMed. The medical field’s rapid evolution—with new treatments, drug interactions, clinical guidelines, and research findings emerging continuously—demands dynamic knowledge updating mechanisms that current RAG architectures cannot accommodate effectively.

Future research must develop incremental learning approaches that can seamlessly integrate new medical knowledge without requiring complete system retraining. This involves solving several technical challenges: (1) maintaining knowledge consistency when updating subsets of the corpus, (2) preserving retrieval performance while incorporating new information, (3) handling conflicting or outdated information as medical understanding evolves, and (4) ensuring that knowledge updates maintain the evidence hierarchies essential for clinical decision-making.

The development of temporal knowledge graphs that can track the evolution of medical understanding, incorporate confidence scores based on evidence quality, and automatically deprecate outdated information represents a significant research frontier. Systems must also address the challenge of integrating real-world evidence from electronic health records with published literature while maintaining appropriate evidence weighting.

#### 7.1.3 Federated learning and privacy-preserving RAG

Medical data privacy requirements create fundamental tensions with the comprehensive knowledge access needed for effective RAG systems. While systems like LiVersa \[ [87](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR87 "Ge J et al (2024) Development of a liver disease-specific large language model chat interface using retrieval augmented generation. Hepatology 10–1097")\] demonstrate PHI-compliant deployment within restricted environments, the broader challenge of enabling knowledge sharing across institutions while preserving patient privacy remains unsolved.

Future work must explore federated RAG architectures where multiple medical institutions can contribute to and benefit from shared knowledge systems without exposing sensitive patient information. This requires developing privacy-preserving retrieval mechanisms, secure multi-party computation for knowledge graph construction, and differential privacy approaches for maintaining utility while protecting individual patient data.

The technical challenges include developing distributed embedding spaces that can be updated across institutions without sharing raw data, creating secure query mechanisms that prevent inference attacks while maintaining retrieval quality, and establishing trust and governance frameworks for federated medical knowledge systems.

### 7.2 Clinical integration and validation challenges

#### 7.2.1 Standardized evaluation frameworks

The literature reveals significant inconsistencies in evaluation approaches across RAG implementations. While some studies like Ke et al. \[ [106](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR106 "Ke YH et al (2025) Retrieval augmented generation for 10 large language models and its generalizability in assessing medical fitness. NPJ Digit Med 8:187")\] employ structured frameworks (S.C.O.R.E. for Safety, Consensus, Objectivity, Reproducibility, Explainability), many rely on manual evaluations or domain-specific metrics that limit cross-system comparisons.

Future work must establish standardized evaluation frameworks that can assess RAG systems across multiple dimensions relevant to clinical deployment: accuracy, safety, explainability, bias, reliability, and user acceptance. These frameworks must account for the unique requirements of different medical specialties while enabling meaningful performance comparisons.

The development of benchmark datasets that reflect real-world clinical complexity, including rare diseases, multi-morbidity, and complex patient presentations, is essential. Current benchmarks often focus on single-condition scenarios that don’t reflect clinical reality where patients present with multiple interacting conditions.

#### 7.2.2 Regulatory approval and clinical validation

The path from research prototype to clinically deployed medical AI system involves regulatory approval processes that current RAG systems have not navigated. The high performance demonstrated by systems like RAG2 \[ [108](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR108 "Sohn J et al (2024) Rationale-guided retrieval augmented generation for medical question answering. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2411.00300                                    ")\] (91.1% accuracy with GPT-4o) and accGPT \[ [93](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR93 "Rau A et al (2023) A context-based chatbot surpasses radiologists and generic ChatGPT in following the ACR appropriateness guidelines. Radiology 308:e230970")\] (82% appropriateness vs. 64–74% human radiologists) suggests clinical utility, but regulatory frameworks for evaluating retrieval-augmented systems remain underdeveloped.

Future work must address the unique challenges of validating systems that combine parametric knowledge (within the LLM) with nonparametric knowledge (retrieved documents). Regulatory bodies require explainable decision-making processes, but current RAG systems often cannot provide complete traceability of how retrieved information influences final outputs.

The development of RAG systems that can meet FDA or equivalent regulatory requirements involves creating audit trails for retrieved information, ensuring reproducibility across different knowledge bases, and demonstrating safety across diverse patient populations and clinical scenarios.

#### 7.2.3 Human-AI collaboration models

The discrepancy between technical performance and physician preference observed in Almanac \[ [88](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR88 "Zakka C et al (2024) Almanac–retrieval-augmented language models for clinical medicine. NEJM AI 1:AIoa2300068")\] (superior accuracy but 57% preference for ChatGPT) highlights the gap between technical optimization and clinical usability. Future work must focus on developing human-AI collaboration models that leverage RAG capabilities while addressing clinician workflow integration.

This involves designing interaction paradigms where clinicians can understand and trust RAG system recommendations, developing interfaces that allow physicians to inspect and validate retrieved evidence, and creating training programs that help medical professionals effectively utilize RAG-enhanced systems.

The challenge extends beyond user interface design to fundamental questions about medical decision-making responsibility, liability when AI systems provide incorrect recommendations based on retrieved information, and the evolution of medical education to incorporate AI collaboration skills.

### 7.3 Technical scalability and efficiency challenges

#### 7.3.1 Computational resource optimization

Current high-performing RAG systems often require substantial computational resources. While CLEAR \[ [125](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR125 "Lopez I et al (2025) Clinical entity augmented retrieval for clinical information extraction. NPJ Digit Med 8:45")\] demonstrates 70% token reduction and 72% inference time improvement, many advanced systems like MedGraphRAG \[ [120](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR120 "Wu J, Zhu J, Qi Y (2024) Medical graph rag: towards safe medical large language model via graph retrieval-augmented generation. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2408.04187                                    ")\] and Omni-RAG \[ [124](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR124 "Chen Z et al (2025) Towards OMNI-RAG: comprehensive retrieval-augmented generation for large language models in medical applications. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2501.02460                                    ")\] require complex processing pipelines that may limit deployment in resource-constrained medical settings.

Future work must address the trade-offs between system capability and computational efficiency. This includes developing lightweight RAG architectures that can provide clinical-grade performance on modest hardware, optimizing retrieval algorithms for medical knowledge bases, and creating adaptive systems that can scale performance based on available computational resources.

The development of edge-deployable RAG systems for medical applications in low-resource settings, emergency medicine, or point-of-care scenarios represents a significant technical challenge requiring novel approaches to knowledge compression, efficient retrieval, and model optimization.

#### 7.3.2 Knowledge base construction and maintenance

The manual curation required for high-performing domain-specific systems like GastroBot \[ [134](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR134 "Zhou Q et al (2024) GastroBot: a Chinese gastrointestinal disease chatbot based on the retrieval-augmented generation. Front Med 11:1392555")\] (25 clinical guidelines, 40 research articles) and CaLM \[ [115](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR115 "Parmanto B et al (2024) A reliable and accessible caregiving language model (CALM) to support tools for caregivers: development and evaluation study. JMIR Format Res 8:e54633")\] (8,568 documents chunked into 196,926 segments) presents scalability challenges for broader medical domain coverage.

Future research must develop automated or semi-automated approaches for medical knowledge base construction that can maintain the quality standards required for clinical applications. This involves natural language processing techniques for automatic document classification, quality assessment, and relationship extraction from medical literature.

The challenge includes developing systems that can automatically identify authoritative sources, extract structured knowledge from unstructured medical texts, resolve conflicts between sources, and maintain knowledge base consistency as new information becomes available.

### 7.4 Ethical and bias mitigation challenges

#### 7.4.1 Healthcare equity and bias prevention

Medical RAG systems risk perpetuating or amplifying existing biases present in medical literature and training data. While some studies acknowledge bias concerns (MMed-RAG \[ [103](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR103 "Xia P et al (2024) MMed-RAG: versatile multimodal rag system for medical vision language models. arXiv preprint                    arXiv:2410.13085                                    ")\] mentions bias mitigation needs), comprehensive approaches to ensuring healthcare equity in RAG systems remain underdeveloped.

Future work must address representation bias in medical knowledge bases, ensuring that retrieved information reflects diverse patient populations, cultural contexts, and global medical practices. This involves developing bias detection mechanisms for medical retrieval systems, creating diverse and representative knowledge bases, and establishing fairness metrics specific to medical applications.

The challenge extends to addressing historical biases in medical research, ensuring that RAG systems don’t perpetuate disparities in healthcare quality, and developing approaches that can identify and mitigate bias in real-time during system operation.

#### 7.4.2 Transparency and explainability requirements

Medical decision-making requires transparent reasoning processes that patients and physicians can understand and validate. While systems like TrialGPT \[ [99](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR99 "Jin Q et al (2023) Matching patients to clinical trials with large language models.                    arXiv:2307.15051                                    ")\] provide natural language explanations for patient-trial matching, comprehensive explainability for complex RAG reasoning remains challenging.

Future research must develop explainable AI techniques specifically designed for medical RAG systems, including visualization approaches for knowledge retrieval processes, natural language explanation generation that physicians can validate, and user interfaces that allow inspection of retrieved evidence and reasoning chains.

The technical challenge involves balancing system performance with explainability requirements, ensuring that explanation systems don’t become additional sources of error, and developing explanation approaches that are meaningful to different stakeholders (patients, physicians, regulators).

### 7.5 Domain-specific research directions

#### 7.5.1 Rare disease and long-tail medical knowledge

Current RAG systems often perform well on common medical conditions but struggle with rare diseases or unusual presentations. CliniqIR \[ [110](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR110 "Abdullahi T, Mercurio L, Singh R, Eickhoff C (2024) Retrieval-based diagnostic decision support: mixed methods study. JMIR Med Inform 12:e50209")\]’s focus on rare disease diagnosis represents early work in this area, but significant challenges remain in developing systems that can effectively retrieve and reason about low-prevalence conditions.

Future work must address the unique challenges of rare disease medicine: limited literature, small patient populations, heterogeneous presentations, and the need for expert knowledge that may not be well-represented in standard medical databases. This involves developing specialized retrieval strategies for rare disease literature, incorporating case report databases, and creating systems that can reason about disease relationships and similarities.

#### 7.5.2 Global health and cross-cultural medicine

The focus on Western medical literature in current systems limits applicability in global health contexts. Duan et al. \[ [130](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11666-9#ref-CR130 "Duan Y et al (2025) Research on a traditional Chinese medicine case-based question-answering system integrating large language models and knowledge graphs. Front Med 11:1512329")\]’s work on Traditional Chinese Medicine RAG systems represents important progress, but broader integration of diverse medical traditions and practices remains unexplored.

Future research must address the integration of traditional medicine knowledge systems, cultural factors in medical decision-making, and the development of RAG systems that can function effectively across different healthcare systems and resource levels. This involves creating multilingual medical knowledge bases, developing cross-cultural medical reasoning capabilities, and addressing the unique challenges of medical AI deployment in low and middle-income countries.

#### 7.5.3 Personalized medicine integration

Current RAG systems primarily retrieve population-level medical knowledge, but the future of medicine increasingly requires personalized approaches based on individual patient characteristics, genetics, lifestyle, and preferences. Future work must explore how RAG systems can integrate personalized patient data with population-level medical knowledge to provide individualized recommendations.

This involves developing privacy-preserving approaches for incorporating personal health records into retrieval processes, creating personalized knowledge graphs that combine individual patient data with medical literature, and addressing the ethical and technical challenges of personalized medical AI systems.

The technical challenges include developing retrieval mechanisms that can identify relevant population subgroups for individual patients, integrating genomic and phenotypic data with medical literature, and creating systems that can adapt recommendations based on individual patient contexts while maintaining evidence-based foundations.

## 8 Conclusion

This Survey thoroughly analyzed and classified RAG model architectures, particularly the Naive, Advanced, and Modular RAG frameworks, to understand their role in enhancing healthcare-specific natural language processing (NLP). Each model was assessed on its ability to retrieve relevant data from external sources and generate meaningful responses. Naive RAG was recognized as the foundational approach, while Advanced RAG introduced more sophisticated indexing and retrieval techniques to improve accuracy. Modular RAG was identified as the most flexible, incorporating specialized modules and customizable components to address domain-specific challenges in healthcare, such as fine-tuning retrieval based on complex medical data structures and enabling sequential, task-oriented processing.

The effectiveness of RAG models in healthcare is grounded in high-quality, domain-specific datasets and robust Large Language Models (LLMs) fine-tuned for medical contexts. When these LLMs are augmented with retrieval capabilities, they bridge the gap between static knowledge and real-time, verified medical information, mitigating hallucinations and enhancing response accuracy. This synergy between medical datasets and fine-tuned LLMs empowers RAG models to deliver contextually relevant, clinically reliable responses, reinforcing their potential as vital tools in advancing healthcare AI applications.

metadata.dc.title: A survey on retrieval-augmentation generation (RAG) models for healthcare applications
metadata.og:description: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) models have become crucial in healthcare applications, significantly enhancing the relevance and reliability of AI-driven insights by combining the generative capabilities of large language models (LLMs) with retrieval-based methods. As healthcare data demand precision and accountability, RAG models address critical limitations of LLMs, such as the tendency to “hallucinate” or produce inaccurate information—by incorporating real-time retrieval from trusted medical knowledge bases and clinical literature. This dual process of retrieving and generating ensures that responses are both contextually accurate and aligned with the latest clinical evidence, making RAG models especially valuable for medical question answering, diagnostics, and treatment planning. This paper presents an in-depth exploration of RAG models and LLMs, specifically within healthcare contexts, to meet the unique demands of medical data processing and decision support. It reviews various RAG architectures, including Naive, Advanced, and Modular RAG approaches, discussing how each framework optimizes retrieval depth, response quality, and computational efficiency. By reducing errors in patient-care recommendations, RAG models play an essential role in scenarios that require high precision and accountability. Additionally, this survey addresses the ethical considerations and transparency requirements for deploying RAG models in healthcare, identifying current challenges and future directions, such as enhancing source interpretability and adapting RAG frameworks for specialized medical fields. By systematically analyzing RAG techniques, this paper provides a comprehensive guide to the state-of-the-art in RAG applications within healthcare, positioning RAG models as a transformative tool for advancing AI-supported healthcare outcomes.

As healthcare data demand precision and accountability, RAG models address critical limitations of LLMs, such as the tendency to “hallucinate” or produce inaccurate information—by incorporating real-time retrieval from trusted medical knowledge bases and clinical literature. This dual process of retrieving and generating ensures that responses are both contextually accurate and aligned with the latest clinical evidence, making RAG models especially valuable for medical question answering, diagnostics, and treatment planning. This paper presents an in-depth exploration of RAG models and LLMs, specifically within healthcare contexts, to meet the unique demands of medical data processing and decision support. It reviews various RAG architectures, including Naive, Advanced, and Modular RAG approaches, discussing how each framework optimizes retrieval depth, response quality, and computational efficiency. By reducing errors in patient-care recommendations, RAG models play an essential role in scenarios that require high precision and accountability. Additionally, this survey addresses the ethical considerations and transparency requirements for deploying RAG models in healthcare, identifying current challenges and future directions, such as enhancing source interpretability and adapting RAG frameworks for specialized medical fields. By systematically analyzing RAG techniques, this paper provides a comprehensive guide to the state-of-the-art in RAG applications within healthcare, positioning RAG models as a transformative tool for advancing AI-supported healthcare outcomes.

metadata.dcDescription: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) models have become crucial in healthcare applications, significantly enhancing the relevance and reliability of AI-driven insights by combining the generative capabilities of large language models (LLMs) with retrieval-based methods. As healthcare data demand precision and accountability, RAG models address critical limitations of LLMs, such as the tendency to “hallucinate” or produce inaccurate information—by incorporating real-time retrieval from trusted medical knowledge bases and clinical literature. This dual process of retrieving and generating ensures that responses are both contextually accurate and aligned with the latest clinical evidence, making RAG models especially valuable for medical question answering, diagnostics, and treatment planning. This paper presents an in-depth exploration of RAG models and LLMs, specifically within healthcare contexts, to meet the unique demands of medical data processing and decision support. It reviews various RAG architectures, including Naive, Advanced, and Modular RAG approaches, discussing how each framework optimizes retrieval depth, response quality, and computational efficiency. By reducing errors in patient-care recommendations, RAG models play an essential role in scenarios that require high precision and accountability. Additionally, this survey addresses the ethical considerations and transparency requirements for deploying RAG models in healthcare, identifying current challenges and future directions, such as enhancing source interpretability and adapting RAG frameworks for specialized medical fields. By systematically analyzing RAG techniques, this paper provides a comprehensive guide to the state-of-the-art in RAG applications within healthcare, positioning RAG models as a transformative tool for advancing AI-supported healthcare outcomes.

## Abstract

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems that answer questions from document collections face compounding difficulties when high-precision citations are required: flat chunking strategies sacrifice document structure, single-query formulations miss relevant passages through vocabulary mismatch, and single-pass inference produces stochastic answers that vary in both content and citation selection. We present KohakuRAG, a hierarchical RAG framework that preserves document structure through a four-level tree representation (document →\\rightarrow section →\\rightarrow paragraph →\\rightarrow sentence) with bottom-up embedding aggregation, improves retrieval coverage through an LLM-powered query planner with cross-query reranking, and stabilizes answers through ensemble inference with abstention-aware voting. We evaluate on the WattBot 2025 Challenge, a benchmark requiring systems to answer technical questions from 32 documents with ±\\pm0.1% numeric tolerance and exact source attribution. KohakuRAG achieves first place on both public and private leaderboards (final score 0.861), as the only team to maintain the top position across both evaluation partitions. Ablation studies reveal that prompt ordering (+80% relative), retry mechanisms (+69%), and ensemble voting with blank filtering (+1.2pp) each contribute substantially, while hierarchical dense retrieval alone matches hybrid sparse-dense approaches (BM25 adds only +3.1pp). We release KohakuRAG as open-source software at https://github.com/KohakuBlueleaf/KohakuRAG.

7Conclusion

WepresentedKohakuRAG,ahierarchicalRAGframeworkthataddressesthreelimitationsofstandardRAG

fordocument-groundedquestionanswering:structurelossfromﬂatchunking(viafour-leveltreeindexingwith

bottom-upembeddingpropagation),query–documentvocabularymismatch(viaLLM-poweredmulti-query

planningwithcross-queryreranking),andanswerinstabilityfromsingle-inferencestochasticity(viaensemble

votingwithabstention-awareblankﬁltering).Ablationstudiesshowthatpromptorderinghasoutsized

impact(+80%fromcontext-before-questionplacement),thattheretrymechanismeﬀectivelyaddresses

unnecessaryabstention(thedominanterrormodeat26.8%offailures)with+69%relativeimprovement,and

thathierarchicaldenseretrievalalonesuﬃcesforcitation-heavytasks(BM25addsonly+3.1pp).Cross-model

ensemblesproviderobustnessagainstdistributionshiftbetweenevaluationpartitions.OntheWattBot2025

Challenge,KohakuRAGachievedﬁrstplaceonboththepublicandprivateleaderboardswithaﬁnalscore

of0.861.Notably,weweretheonlyteamtomaintainthetoppositionacrossbothpartitionswhileother

competitivesubmissionsshowedscoredegradationupto−0.046.

Limitations.Severallimitationswarrantfutureinvestigation.Ourrule-baseddocumentparsingrelies

onheuristicsforsectiondetectionandmayfailonirregularlayouts.Referencemismatcherrors(23.6%of

failures)indicatethatLLMsstrugglewithpreciseattributionevenwhenanswersarecorrect,oftenciting

surveypapersratherthanoriginalsources.Ensembleinferenceincurslinearcostscalingwiththenumberof

runs.Theretrymechanismimprovesrecallbutincreaseslatencyfordiﬃcultquestions.Promisingdirections

includelearneddocumentparsing,reference-constraineddecoding,andconﬁdence-basedadaptiveretrieval

thatpredictswhenadditionalcontextisneeded.

A.1IndexingPipeline

Thissectiondescribesthedocumentparsing,embeddingcomputation,andvectorstoragecomponentsthat

DocumentParsing.Documentparsingemploysarule-basedpipelinetailoredfortechnicalPDFsand

contains:

•id:Hierarchicalidentiﬁer(e.g.,doc1:sec2:p3:s4)

•level:Oneof{document,section,paragraph,sentence}

•content:Rawtextcontent

•children:ListofchildnodeIDs

•metadata:Sourceﬁle,pagenumber,boundingbox(forimages)

PDFTextExtraction.TextextractionusesPyMuPDF’s

method,whichreturns

QueryPlanning.Thequeryplannerusesastructuredpromptrequestingndiversequeries:

Giventhequestion:{question}

Thiscreatesaninterestingevaluationdynamic:thepublicleaderboardmeasuresperformanceononetest

distributionthatteamscanindirectlyoptimizethroughsubmissionfeedback,whiletheprivateleaderboard

measurestransfertoadiﬀerenttestdistributionwithnooptimizationsignal.Arobustmethodshouldperform

wellonbothpartitions,astheﬁnalevaluationconsidersaggregateperformanceacrosstheentiretestset.

[Retrieval-Augmented Generation for AI-Generated Content: A Survey](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/399398595_Retrieval-Augmented_Generation_for_AI-Generated_Content_A_Survey)

Article

Full-text available

- Jan 2026

- [Penghao Zhao](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Penghao-Zhao-2275612662)
- [Hailin Zhang](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Hailin-Zhang-2209793748)
- [![Qinhan Yu](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Qinhan Yu](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Qinhan-Yu-3)
- [![Bin Cui](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Bin Cui](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Bin-Cui-6)

Advancements in model algorithms, the growth of foundational models, and access to high-quality datasets have propelled the evolution of Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC). Despite its notable successes, AIGC still faces hurdles such as updating knowledge, handling long-tail data, mitigating data leakage, and managing high training and inference costs. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has recently emerged as a paradigm to address such challenges. In particular, RAG introduces the information retrieval process, which enhances the generation process by retrieving relevant objects from available data stores, leading to higher accuracy and better robustness. In this paper, we comprehensively review existing efforts that integrate RAG techniques into AIGC scenarios. We first classify RAG foundations according to how the retriever augments the generator, distilling the fundamental abstractions of the augmentation methodologies for various retrievers and generators. This unified perspective encompasses all RAG scenarios, illuminating advancements and pivotal technologies that help with potential future progress. We also summarize additional enhancement methods for RAG, facilitating effective engineering and implementation of RAG systems. Then from another view, we survey practical applications of RAG across different modalities and tasks, offering valuable references for researchers and practitioners. Furthermore, we introduce the benchmarks for RAG, discuss the limitations of current RAG systems, and suggest potential directions for future research.
